input text:
Most people are accepting of you having an anxiety disorder, but an unfortunate amount of people expect you to only have symptoms in private. A stigma that I’ve noticed in the mental health community... so many people are happy to talk about it and embrace your differences, but oh no.. don’t have a panic attack in front of them, don’t seek re-assurance from today’s anxious thoughts and jitters. 

This is something that needs changing. It is more than ok for someone with diabetes to be comforted even when their their blood sugar gets too low and it affects their work. Even if the circumstances of the blip in their health was due to poor diet or a rough week (I had a co-worker that had this happen at work) but when I struggled with low mood I was told ‘we all have days like that’ ... ‘don’t let customers see you unhappy or uncomfortable’ 

This is an issue with how subjective everyday anxiety and a chronic mental health issue is, if someone does not understand the effort it takes to behave normally they will default to judging you based on how they deal with it. 

Of course it’s not all bad, I’m lucky to have family and a handful of friends that have seen me go though treatments and recovery so know the full spectrum of my issues and do not judge or baby me. 

I felt like sharing this little rant to hear your thoughts on if you’ve been accepted or told to ‘hide’ symptoms from the public to spare THEIR discomfort... like your doing it on purpose.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses how people with diabetes (and mental health issues) are expected to hide symptoms to avoid discomforting others, reflecting perceived and anticipated stigma

input text:
I Lied About My Weight Loss—Feeling Guilty and Stuck I’ve been on Zepbound since early August and have lost 40 lbs. I feel amazing—easily the best decision I’ve ever made for my health. I’ve only told my SIL and one other person who has also taken Zep.

Last night, I had dinner with a close friend who asked about the 75-day challenge I did (which I posted a lot about). It involves daily workouts, drinking a gallon of water, and eating clean. In her mind, that’s how I lost the weight. I just kept talking about how much better I feel and didn’t mention Zep at all.

Then she made a comment about how crazy it is to see people lose so much weight on Ozempic and joked that after she has her baby, she’s going to try it. That was my moment to tell her—but instead, I just said, “You definitely can! And do the exercise challenge too! Nothing wrong with that!”

Then she brought up concerns, saying so many people are on it now and what if in 10 years it causes cancer or something. I told her that people have been taking these meds for diabetes for years and that I don’t think anything major like that will come out. But I still froze on telling her the truth about my own journey.

I know Zep isn’t cheating, but I really don’t want the judgment or side comments about it. I don’t think she would judge me, but I worry that since I’ve posted so much about the challenge, it looks like that’s all it took for me to lose weight. If people find out I’m on meds, will they diminish the hard work I’ve done?

It’s really bothering me that I haven’t told her. I just wish the stigma was gone so I could shout it from the rooftops! The truth is, since the challenge ended a month ago, I haven’t lost much more weight, which tells me Zep alone isn’t enough—you still have to put in the effort. But I still worry about the judgment.

Ugh! What do y’all think I should do?
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author expresses fear of judgment and stigma if others learn they use diabetes medication (Zepbound/Ozempic) for weight loss, perceives negative

input text: Are non diabetics changing the way the public see cgms? Hi, so I’m a t1D and I work at a cafe. I’m on a cgm and an insulin pump so I’m used to (and mostly okay) with comments and questions about it, however, I have been asked maybe 8 times in the last two weeks if I’m wearing a ZOE. I had no idea what it was so I searched it and it seems to be a cgm for non t1ds to get nutrition information from. The first few times I would just laugh and go ‘oh haha no I’m type one diabetic’ and then move on but today a man was pretty insistent that it was one. I tried my best to explain calmly a multitude of times and then he said ‘you don’t have to hide it’. UGH. I am ultimately glad that medical/non medical wearable devices are seen more often as it takes some of the stigma away but after being diabetic for over 19 years nothing is more frustrating to me then someone who has their ears switched off to the importance of it. I’m not sure if anyone else had had this comparison before but what do we think this means for diabetics? ZOE sounds like a useful device for a non diabetic but are things like ZOE starting to downplay why we actually HAVE to wear our devices?
label: Yes stigma

input text:
Girlfriend was recently diagnosed. Any advice is welcome For context, my gf (22F) was telling me for weeks that she couldn’t get this sweet taste out of her mouth, and that she had severe dry mouth. We thought nothing of it other than maybe scheduling a dentist appointment. Then came the frequent urination which we thought was just her body expelling the extra water the dry mouth was causing her to drink. But then she got very sick. I found her on the floor of my bathroom one afternoon barely responsive and took her to urgent care. Her urine came back with sugar in it, and they tested her blood immediately. Her sugar was 483. Needless to say, she left in an ambulance on the doctor’s advice and spent almost a week in the hospital. For a couple days she was still in DKA, but they closed her gap and have sent her home with insulin and a completely changed life. Until this point, we had no idea she had diabetes. She eats healthy, weighs 110 pounds, exercises, etc. 

Since this, our lives have completely changed. I was by her side the entire time, and have been since her discharge from the hospital a week ago. She is so depressed, hopeless, and ruined by this. She hates counting carbs, injecting herself, having to live with the stigma of being a diabetic. I’m watching the joy fade from her eyes every day and most of the time it seems like my happy-go-lucky girl is gone. 

How do you guys cope? What are some tips for managing a new diabetes diagnosis (it was definitely diagnosed as type 1)? What are some zero carb / low carb snacks that are actually good and will make her happy? 

Anything helps. Thanks so much for reading.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The girlfriend feels "ruined" and "hopeless" by her diagnosis and "hates...having to live with the stigma of being a diabetic," indicating internalized shame

input text:
For those who developed Type 2 through lifestyle choices, how do you handle the psychological fallout? It's been hard for me to process this lately. I know there is a lot of stigma around this sensitive topic; however, for me, there were preventable lifestyle factors that probably caused my diabetes. I hate that my condition is probably largely preventable, given my circumstances.

I had a sedentary lifestyle. I ate a lot of processed foods. I loss a lot of muscle mass. I ate high refined carbs and didn't exercise afterwards. All these lifestyle factors most likely contributed to the development of this metabolic problem.

And I guess it's not just this one area, it's a pattern throughout my life. As I aged over 40, I am realizing there are a lot of problems in my life that are preventable, that I did to myself. I can't help but think of myself derogatorily. I know that my family has a history of diabetes and there might be some genetic predisposition, but I largely blame myself. I think the blame is warranted, too.

Anyone else out there like me? It's just turning into a lot of self-hatred, especially when a new problem emerges. A lot of self-blame. I don't really know how to just let it go and move on. I'm curious how others deal with it, or they just don't have this issue.

Thanks.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text expresses self-blame, shame, and self-hatred for developing Type 2 diabetes, attributing it to personal lifestyle choices and describing these feelings as "warranted."

input text:
100 Reasons Not To Have Kids (I thought this was gonna be hard but it wasn't) I thought this was gonna be hard but i literally started firing these off at lightning speed. I found this blog where a mother of 12 lists [100 Reasons to Have  and it had me and my sister losing our minds. Some of the reasons were completely untrue, easily disputed, only a mild plus at best, or didn't require a child for at all

Example: *Have a child so you have an excuse to grab a lolly-pop at the bank.* JUST GRAB THE LOLLIPOP KIM YOU DON'T NEED TO BIRTH A HUMAN TO DO THAT!!!! XD my other favorite was, *Have children so you can save money on birth control* WHAT?!?!?! FUCKING WHAT! KIM, I THINK THE $12 ON BC EACH MONTH IS LIGHT CHANGE COMPARED TO THE   x 12 YOU'LL BE DEALING WITH!!!!

Anyway here's a list of 100 reasons to not have kids.

;#x200B;

1. **Migraines.** Even worse I get ocular migraines, which means not only am I in skull piercing pain but iIm blinded too. It would be absolute hell to have a screaming child with me during a migraine, and horrifying if my child needed help and I was debilitated because of a migraine.
2. **I like things being clean**
3. **I hate being interrupted.** Not necessarily in conversations, but I like to finish things I start. Kids interrupt. I can't watch a tv show without having to pause it 12 times whenever I babysit. I can't do craft projects or clean or even shower without being interrupted at some point.
4. **They bite.**
5. **You literally have no idea what kind of person you're gonna get.**
6. **higher chance of** **~~divorce~~** **marital dissatisfaction.**
7. **Probably shouldn't curse anymore**
8. **You'll get dirty looks if you have a kid and curse**
9. **get dirty looks from people in public if you're child isn't behaving**
10. **no alcohol either**
11. **Children have no filter**: they'll embarrass you and tell people things you don't want being broadcast.
12. **They might even get you in legal trouble.** (harming another child, stealing, calling 911 for no reason, saying something troubling or out of context (ex: My sister told the school that my mom did cocaine when my mom actually just smoked and my sister confused nicotine with cocaine)
13. **"Your children will always love you" is a bold face lie.** (this one's partly from the article linked above.) Being a parent doesn't mean you'll always have a good relationship with you're child.
14. **"Children are always so sweet." NOPE.** (paraphrased but also from the article) I work daycare and I've seen toddlers look their parents in the eyes and tell them "I hate you", "Go away!", "You're not fun!", "I don't want to go with you, I want to go with\_\_\_\_", etc. Kids can be very sweet, but they're also just as likely to kick you in the dick when you're already down. Quiet literally for my brother.
15. **Children (especially toddlers) flip-flop emotions like crazy.** It's so draining. one minute they love you, the next they're saying you're not their friend and they want you to die.
16. **overwhelmed by the pressure of being a perfect parent.**
17. **having a child is literally one of the highest factors in poverty.** If you have a child while still living under the poverty line you are WAY more unlikely to ever pull yourself out of it. Also, having children is one of the main reasons people fall into poverty in the first place.
18. **trips are more complicated**
19. **traveling in general is more complicated**
20. **vacations aren't as relaxing with kids**
21. **literally every freak'n decision you will ever make for the rest of your life is now more complicated**
22. **for better or for worse, having children will completely alter your life. Nothing will be the same ever again**
23. **having to leave or miss out on weddings/events because of fussy babies**
24. **feeling like you're missing out on living because you had a child too young or too quick** (this is something i see a lot actually, and it's really sad.
25. **if you had kids before college, you probably aren't going to college (or it will be much harder to do it)**
26. **giving up your dreams**
27. **permanent ear damage**
28. **shortened life expectancy**
29. **Less sleep/sleep deprivation**
30. **You shouldn't have a child if you're not financially stable**
31. **You shouldn't have a child if you're not emotionally stable**
32. **You shouldn't have a child if you're not mentally stable**
33. **permanent scaring (potential c-sections + sometimes children just thrown shit at you or bite you)**
34. **Stretch marks** (honestly, I don't really care about this one. I know a lot of people with stretch marks: some rock them, but others are really insecure about them. Personally, I don't think they're ugly.)
35. **gestational diabetes**
36. **Pre-Eclampsia**
37. **tearing (seriously i cringe every time i hear it)**
38. **amniotic rupture**
39. **Health risks in general, etc**
40. **Most people let themselves go (health wise) after having kids**
41. **not wanting to pass on genetic illness**
42. **not being emotionally prepared to care for a child with mental illness**
43. **not being physically prepared to care for a child with mental illness**
44. **not being financially prepared to care for a child with mental illness**
45. **not being emotionally prepared to deal with a child with physical illness**
46. **not being physically prepared to deal with a child with physical illness**
47. **not being financially prepared to deal with a child with physical illness**
48. **not being emotionally able to care for a child with disabilities**
49. **not being physically able to care for a child with disabilities**
50. **not being financially able to care for a child with disabilities**
51. **if you wouldn't be able to accept a child born differently**
52. **Your standards for living often drop**
53. **are you ready to have to deal with babies pulling out a fistful of their own shit and painting the walls with it?** (happened to my brother)
54. **Are you ready for a toddler to grab a fist full of their own shit and try to throw it at you/put it in your mouth?** (also happened to my brother)
55. **Not being able to relax when you get home**
56. **parenthood IS a full time job**
57. **having the most stressful day of your life and coming home to more stress**
58. **explosive diarrhea**
59. **changing diapers** (get used to changing them. every child learns at a different rate. even at four, my niece still needs pull ups at night. Kids can be 5, 6, and 7 and still be having potty issues.)
60. **you gotta buy SO MANY DIAPERS $$$**
61. **potty training.** It sucks and often takes many years (could take up to 5 or more since many children have trouble not peeing the bed)
62. **The tax benefit isn't worth it.** You get a tax benefit from having children, however you have to spend colossally more money overall to have a child, so it doesn't negate from all the money you've already spent.
63. **being permanently and legally tied to the other parent for the rest of your life.** (I've actually heard a lot of stories from people that wanted to be parents and love having children, but they deeply regret the person they had kids with.)
64. **being a single parent.** (it's VERY common. and unbelievably stressful and difficult.)
65. **No presents for you** (this one varies but my brother had a kid at 22 and he was genuinely sad at Christmas when he got no real gifts and all of his gifts were parenting related. maybe it's petty, but damn I'd be sad too if on top of all the stresses of parenthood I didn't get a present.)
66. **Everything costs so much money and it never stops.**
67. **the expenses for all the baby stuff**
68. **+ years of presents for every child at least twice a year**
69. **+ years of paying for school stuff**
70. **+ years of paying for extracurricular activity stuff**
71. **+ every time they want to hang out with friends and need money**
72. **+ every doctor visit** (because capitalism)
73. **+ every dental visit** (same)
74. **+ times when they just walk in and demand money**
75. **also you're grocery bill will be MUCH larger for the next 20 years**
76. **having more babies creates more pollution.**
77. **The more kids you have the larger the carbon footprint you leave, and that isn't good.**
78. **watch your children age and become increasingly aware of your own age and mortality in the process**
79. **drowning in the depressing knowledge that you kids are growing up and will leave the nest.**
80. **getting divorced/ being single and experiencing your kids moving out of state or out of country leaving you entirely alone**
81. **experiencing the death of a child is one of the most soul crushing things you can endure. Also it often destroys your marriage and leaves your life in shambles.**
82. **the guilt of having a child and them becoming a criminal and everyone putting part of the blame on you**
83. **having children does not guarantee someone will take care of you when you're old** (they might move far away, die, be in jail, or just not like you enough to help)
84. **pregnancy**
85. **childbirth**
86. **postpartum depression** (it kills more woman than you think. Also sometimes the change in hormones doesn't correct itself and you end up having lifelong hormonal issues following pregnancy.)
87. **having children is a colossal commitment, so if you're afraid of commitment, or just like having the freedom to do as you do, having children could be a huge mistake.**
88. **A human child is literally the most high maintenance thing you could ever have to care for.**
89. **humans are over populated.**
90. **Human pollution is literally destroying the earth right now**
91. **Kids are loud. I like silence.**
92. **Introverted. I feel so unbelievable drained after social gathers or events. I'd feel exhausted at all times with a kid.**
93. **kids need consent attention (especially little ones) It's so draining to me.**
94. **Feel like wasting time.** I'll be honest, this is a me thing. I hate feeling dragged into going to something I don't care about. Open house? school plays? watching little kids play basketball? I've always hated going to those things.
95. **I could be doing something productive.** Might come off a little shitty, but there have been multiple occasions when watching kids for my family where i was just sitting there thinking about all the more productive things i wish i was doing instead. It made me realize that mothering kids felt like a chore to me rather than something i enjoy.
96. **What if you have a child only to realize it's not what you want at all?** There's a lot of parental regret going on, but many people don't feel comfortable admitting that they regret their choice of parenthood, because there is such a stigma in admitting you're unhappy as a parent. so if you did regret your choice you'd likely feel trapped and incapable of connecting with someone on how you feel leading to sever mental and emotion issues alone with the potential mistreatment of your child.
97. **Parents are more stressed.**
98. **Parents have higher risk of depression.** I already struggle with depression and suicidal thoughts. Adding a kid to mix wouldn't be a good idea at all.
99. **I AM NOT A MORNING PERSON.** having a kid makes you a morning person whether you want to or not.
100. **Why create more human beings when there are hundreds of thousands of children already out there right now that need homes?**
101. **The systems in place (at least where i live) prioritize forcing women to carry to term, but don't help children after they're born. Hospital bills, children with health issue, falling into crippling financial dept due to an inability to meet ridiculous payment demands.**
102. **Not all women are maternal**
103. **Not all people make good parents.**
104. **Some people genuinely hate kids and encouraging those people to be parents is a terrible idea.**
105. **It needs to be thought through.** Children are a huge responsibility, commitment, and source of stress. I have multiple friends and family that work in child services, A sad number of people realize too late that it's not for them and end up ruining their life and their kid's life.
106. **I like having me time.**
107. **I like my hobbies that I'd have to put on hold with kids around**
108. **I don't want to put my career on hold.**
109. **Gender disappointment**
110. **The expectation to dress more modest or more kid friendly**
111. **If you're a pregnant woman you're going to get grabbed and touched inappropriately when you're out in public.**
112. **You have to deal with your kid's friends**
113. **And their friend's parents are now your friends whether you want them to be or not.**
114. **You now have to spend time at places you probably hate.**
115. **You also have to sit and watch movies and shows that are slowly driving you insane.**
116. **You're going to be asked to do very inconvenient favors all the time.**
117. **You're kids might have hobbies or interest that drive you INSANE** (or that you're afraid of like a snake obsession or something. Personally i'm completely down with having some snakes, my sister has on and he's pretty chill, but i know A LOT of people that can't deal with them at all.
118. **I don't like feeling limited or restricted in what I can do.**
119. **Kids should be a choice you make not something you do just because you think it's what you're supposed to do.** A lot of people end in unhappy situations because they had kids without thinking about the implications, responsibilities, and sacrifices involved.
120. **An afternoon siesta is literally the best thing ever.**

input text:
The weight loss portion of my postpartum journey is over! Disclaimer: these pictures were taken in the morning before I had breakfast, the only thing in my belly was the Celsius I had before working out. After a day of eating… yeah, my belly isn’t that flat anymore lol and next week I’ll probably gain 2-3lbs in bloat. 

I delivered in December at the age of 32 and waited until after my 6 week pp appointment to worry about weight loss at all (really, I was closer to 8 weeks pp when I started). Pre-pregnancy, I weighed 131lbs at 5’5”, which is the weight my body just kind of naturally stays at with my normal eating and exercise habits. 

After coming home with baby, I weighed 155lbs, and hardly any more came off over the next few weeks. I wasn’t able to breastfeed due to underproducing and what I WAS producing, my baby hated (a whole other tangent I won’t go on here). I noticed my body felt different (almost as if it just spent the better part of a year building an entire other person, wild) and one day when I was stretching in front of the mirror, I realized I felt weak, like a wet noodle. I wanted to feel strong again, especially knowing I’ll blink and this baby will be a toddler I’ll have to chase everywhere. 

During pregnancy, I had gestational diabetes with insulin controlled fasting numbers (so eat that stigma that comes along with that diagnosis, it can happen to ANYONE and the degree of how bad it is is also just up to the placenta roulette), and I actually learned a lot about food during that time. Prior to that, I honestly just ate whatever, I didn’t pay attention to macros. I’m honestly glad now that I had it, because it changed a lot about how I eat. I focus more now on eating a lot of protein, fiber, and healthy fats, but one hill that I will absolutely die on is that carbs are NOT the enemy. Our bodies need them for energy and function. I didn’t count carbs before and I refuse to count or restrict carbs in any way now. My focus is on eating a very balanced diet and enjoying food still. My favorites are anything Magic Spoon, Quest chips, and Goodles, as well as a good Sola bagel in the mornings. Avocados are magical and go anywhere they can fit in, too. 

As for losing the weight, literally all I’ve done is walk. I walk for one hour/3 miles every morning on a walking pad my husband got me off of Amazon a long time ago while baby is either sleeping, playing in her pack n play by the bed, or acting absolutely feral in her jumper right across from me. If I can go for another walk with baby in the late afternoon, I try to do that too, but not really for the exercise, more to get us both some sunshine and fresh air. I make it a point to get at least 10,000 steps a day although my ideal is really closer to 12,500. 

I’m ready to just tone this up and tone up my arms now, I’m so thankful for this body that grew such a wonderful little person who lights up my life and inspires me to be better to live a long, healthy life for her.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author references the stigma associated with gestational diabetes, explicitly mentioning "eat that stigma that comes along with that diagnosis," and challenges the misconception that it only happens to certain people, indicating awareness

input text:
Why is India still so weird about GLP1 meds? 
GLP1 meds have been around for some time now at least buzz around them has been there for years. Why does it feel like India is still super hesitant when it comes to meds like Ozempic, Mounjaro, etc.? Like, in other countries, people are openly using them (not just for diabetes but weight loss too), but here it’s either hush-hush or straight-up frowned upon.

Doctors hesitate to recommend them unless you’re severely diabetic, and even then, there’s so much resistance. Most people still see it as a “shortcut” or a “luxury,” and don’t get me started on the stigma around using meds for weight loss.

Is it just the cost? Or cultural stuff like “just eat less and walk more”? Or maybe doctors aren’t fully on board yet? Would love to hear what others think.

input text:
How much different would life be for us socially if Type-1 Diabetes was considered a form of lupus instead of diabetes? We get lumped in with the Type-2s as "diabetic," and unfortunately Type-2 Diabetes has a stigma surrounding it because people think it’s a result of bad lifestyle choices. Whether or not this is accurate I won’t take a stand on. The point here isn’t to punch down on the Type-2s. 

Regardless, all of us have at some point received unsolicited (and often patronizing) armchair dietary advice, medical advice, and ignorant comments and jokes about Diary Queen Blizzards, Wilford Brimley and other stuff intended for type-2 diabetics. It’s annoying because they have very little in common, with Type-1 being an autoimmune disease and Type-2 being a metabolic disease. It’s someone saying they’re from Austria, and someone responds with "G’day mate, you call that a knife?"

This misconception is probably going to start having some drastic real life consequences for us with some stuff RFK has been saying as Secretary of Health and Human Services.

Would it be totally out of the question to push for reclassifying Type-1 Diabetes as a form of lupus instead of a form of Diabetes? We can call it "pancreatic lupus" or something. I feel like our lives would be at least 5% easier.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses negative stereotypes and misconceptions about diabetes, especially conflating Type 1 and Type 2, leading to unsolicited advice and

input text:
This is Bullshit Just want to start off by saying that this is the rambling of someone who is both hungry and stressed. I’m trying to get my head on straight when it comes to being diabetic and wanted a space to air out my struggles and see if anyone more experienced has any words of wisdom. Apologies if this isn’t the type of post allowed here, again I’m new. But anyways here is my brain dump full of pissing and moaning:

Absolute horse shit. 

I was diagnosed as diabetic 2 weeks ago and have been struggling with some big emotions ever since. I’ve always known that this was extremely common in my family and it’s haunted me like a boogie man ever since. But damn it everything you see online (and in my family) is that T2D is caused by obesity or it starts later in life. Well I’m not only in better shape but I’m also younger than my dad was when he was diagnosed. (I’ve never been over 180lbs and I’m only 27 for context) I’m not at all trying to contribute to the stigma that this disease is something we do to ourselves but damn it I thought I would have more time before I had to deal with this. Guess not. 

When I was told my a1c came back as 10 I radically changed my diet and reduced my carb intake to a maximum of 40g per day. Which I’ve actually been doing pretty good with, my daily average is closer to 30g a day. So you would think my bg levels are improving right? Well my fasting levels are still over 100 every day if not closer to 150. I actually got my first reading of under 100 this morning at a cool 92. But would you like to know what I ate yesterday? 1,100 calories and 20g of carbs. I went to bed with my stomach growling because I was so frustrated with my blood sugar that I just couldn’t deal with eating anything else no matter the carb count. And you would expect the response to be “oh you can’t starve yourself that’s not good for you either!” But no. I’m type 2 so that means I can ALWAYS control my levels and prevent spikes with diet ONLY. So my struggling is my fault. I decided to give myself this disease. I chose to destroy my body’s ability to process insulin. This whole situation is my fault and I should be able to fix it by just changing what I put in my mouth. 

Well I’m trying that and I get that it’s only been a few weeks but this is still such bullshit. I went to dinner with friends the other day and had a chicken burrito. So chicken, bell peppers, onion, water, and 1(one) tortilla. My blood sugar was over 200 for 3 hours afterwards. What is the point in being so restrictive and disciplined with my diet if a single tortilla can destroy everything else I try? 

I mean sure when I’m being “good” my levels are always in range which is great. But that “good” is a maximum of 40g of carbs per day. So do I just never eat a slice of bread again? Never have pizza, potatoes, carrots, fruit, berries, or anything even remotely sweet ever again? Is that what I have to do to not go blind and lose my feet?

I know that getting unsolicited advice on how to manage this disease is par for the course and I know that I’m still in the adjustment phase but it is so hard for this to feel worth it when any little deviation will destroy my progress. Then to be told that it’s all my fault and I just need to try “harder” is extremely demoralizing. 

I don’t really know what the point of this is I’m just feeling angry and scared I grew up watching my dad slowly deteriorate due to his diabetes and I know I don’t want to go down that path. But it feels damn near inevitable and I don’t know what to do.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author expresses self-blame and shame ("this whole situation is my fault"), perceives that others blame them for their diabetes, and anticipates ongoing judgment

input text:
How am I supposed to do this..? I’m 15 and got diagnosed with type 2 diabetes last Tuesday. And I have no idea what I am doing. I feel really lost and overwhelmed emotionally and physically and my parents are not helping so hello Reddit. I have some questions, and I’d love any advice you folks have.

1. Finger sticks :/
What are your tricks for doing them? I’ve gotten better at them over the past week but I still suck at them. Right now I have to do them 3-4 times a day. 

2. Mental fall out/ grief (?) 
Since I’ve been diagnosed I have felt a range of emotions. It’s worsened my depression, I feel like it’s my fault, and part of me is still in denial that I even have it. It’s also just eating up a lot of my mental space so it’s exhausting me and I don’t know how to just accept it and move on. This part is really affecting me in a lot of ways, my room isn’t clean, I’ve missed some days of school and I’m falling behind. It’s hard to be learning how to manage this condition and also just mentally accepting it at the same time. 
 
3. Food 
Oh my goodness is this a big one. What am I supposed to eat??? Can I eat regular food, do I have to eat special food or what?? How am I supposed to eat too?? Like when I eat sometimes a little bit is enough and sometimes after I feel like a ravenous beast. I’ve thrown up a few times over the past few days from eating and it’s so weird and confusing. Everything online says type 2 can be controlled by diet but there is so so much noise and everyone thinks I’m doing it wrong or has an opinion on how I “should” do it. I also know it’s helpful to track what you eat, but so far I haven’t really found an app yet does anybody have recommendations? 

4. How do I carry all my stuff 
Currently I’m using a toiletries bag which I don’t love cause I need it for when I travel. I have a lot of stuff in it and idk what kind of bag would be the best to use. 

5. When does it start feeling better??
Currently I feel sick and so so tired and not good. I know that the meds will eventually make me feel better but when??

6. How do I beat the stigma??
This one is unfortunately mostly perpetuated by my parents and grandparents who are older.  They’re under the impression that type 2 is preventable and I did this to myself. I’m not overweight, and I know it’s from genetics but I still can’t help internalizing that it’s my fault. I should’ve tried harder or done things different or anything. It’s hard to not feel like people in my life are disappointed and disgusted by me.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The writer expresses internalized stigma (shame, self-blame), perceives negative attitudes from family, anticipates judgment from others, and

input text:
Ethan's refusal to admit he's taking GLP-1 medications would be a really shitty thing... **Not gonna go into too many details but let's just say I'm in the medical field and have a ton of experience with patients on GLP-1 meds over the last 3 yrs. Sorry for the long post!**

In my opinion, Ethan has been taking GLP-1 meds since around the time he started taking Wellbutrin. 

I have personally seen countless patients start with a new medical practice and go through the full battery of tests to determine how the new practice can help them. Since GLP-1 meds have become popular and readily available, it is now common practice to (after testing that these meds would be safe for the patient) prescribe these meds for chronically overweight patients alongside the normally-prescribed anti-depressants, statins, sleep meds, etc... 

*To be clear* I personally believe that these drugs are miraculous. Patients with type 2 and pre-diabetes, hypertension, depression, high cholesterol, low energy -- hell, even patients suffering from chronic pain, have gone into remission after a few short months on these medications. 

Here's how it likely went down: 

Ethan found a new doctor for his depression (very likely something called a "concierge doctor" which is currently all the rage for rich people) and the doctor very likely raved about GLP-1s to Ethan when he talked about his obesity and weight loss struggle. Doctors are *genuinely excited* about GLP-1s because we all see them as **miraculous**. 

So anyway the doctor probably sold Ethan on the drugs (which is a good thing), and Ethan started taking them around the time he started taking his new anti-depressants (another good thing). Here's my evidence: 

* One of the main side effects of GLP-1s are abnormal bowel movements and stomach upset in the form of diarrhea and nausea. 
* A rare (but not unheard-of) side effect of GLP-1s is rash and skin irritation. Most patients I've seen have had it around the injection site, but I've also had a few patients who are allergic to the specific compounding of the GLP-1 prescribed. In these cases it may take one or two changes to another GLP-1 to resolve, but generally it normalizes. 
* Simple diet changes and increased activity do not result in the kind of weight loss Ethan has had during this period, especially when the person is eating the kinds of foods they were while obese. 
* Plateaus happen in all weight loss journeys, however are much less pernicious while patients are using GLP-1s. Ethan's plateau was extremely short, and was much more in-line with what we see with GLP-1s than traditional methods of weight loss. 
* Ethan has shown himself to be extremely knowledgeable about Ozempic and GLP-1 medications, and has spoken about them in the same way that lots of doctors and patients speak about them. 

It's really not up for debate for me, in a similar way it wasn't up for debate with that TikTok doctor. However here's where I disagree with the TikTok doctor...

**Why I think this is harmful**

Obesity and obesity-related diseases currently kill more Americans than all other causes of death combined. We have a real problem with food, eating, and exercise in this country, and it's been a problem since at least the mid-80s. What's worse is that our culture treats obese people like they are enemies. Either they're too lazy, gluttonous, or stupid to get healthy and lose weight, but the average American (even the average OBESE American) views obese people in an extremely negative way. 

Now we're very early in the process here with less than 5 years of real observable data on GLP-1s, but I would happily go so far as to say that whatever harmful side effects that may come from GLP-1s, the benefits far outweigh them. I've seen patients basically on death's door do a complete 180 and become healthier, more active, and HAPPIER people, usually in less than a year. I personally have told obese friends and family members to consult with their doctors about GLP-1s, and every single one who's gone through with it has thanked me for it. 

We should view these medicines the way that we view synthetic insulin. We should view these medicines the way that we view antidepressants. What was once generally a chronic and terminal condition is now extremely treatable. Mocking someone for taking any of these medicines should be seen the same way: cruel and disgusting. 

I understand why Ethan would refuse to admit his use of GLP-1s given how many patients view them. Again, this is a new type of medicine, and with all new medicines we see some social stigma rise up around them. However I personally think as someone in his position he should be brave enough to say:

"hey, I shit-talked Ozempic at first because on some level we're all a little bit fatphobic. Boy was I wrong. These are miracle drugs in the same way that antidepressants are, and I am extremely grateful to have the money that affords me access to them." 

Until he says something like that, I just struggle to feel anything but disdain for the guy. Obviously weight loss is a private journey for all people who take it on, but he's a very public figure with a mouthpiece. He could really help kill the stigma on this thing and actually save lives. 

Rant over.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses widespread negative stereotypes about obesity and diabetes (perceived), acknowledges why someone might hide GLP-1 use due to fear of

input text:
How much insulin do you take? Greetings community. As a T1 diabetic, I take insulin quite often, and I’m just curious to see how insulin reacts for others. As someone with a bit of insulin resistance, I average 20-30 units per day, which was shocking to my mother who only requires about 10. My meal times, based on what I eat, can be 5-10 alone, with a couple units every couple hours or so to maintain. I know there are maaaany factors to consider when dosing that will alter the effectiveness of insulin, but I would like to hear some experiences from other T1s.

EDIT: because this got more responses than I expected! Thank you for sharing. I appreciate having a safe space to have a discussion like this in. I meant no judgments in making this post, and I’ve learned a lot from the comments. Personally, as someone still struggling with balance, it’s helpful to know the experiences of others living with this condition. I know there tends to be a stigma around those who need “higher”doses, and I feel that being able to talk about the normal life of a T1 can lessen that stigma, even if it’s just in our community. I will definitely take your comments into consideration, and will continue to experiment with my own insulin journey.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author acknowledges the existence of stigma around needing "higher" insulin doses and expresses a desire to reduce it by discussing their experiences. This shows awareness of negative societal views (

input text:
5am voting for PWD for election  tried to vote at 5 AM, since I was issued a PWD card due to my mental health condition. However, the teacher there made me leave the line. When I showed my PWD ID, she said, “We don’t even issue those.” But PWD IDs aren’t issued in schools or precincts anyway. Then she added, “Are you sure it’s not just PCOS?” when I mentioned that I see a psychiatrist. I didn’t even disclose my diagnoses—yet she was already making assumptions, probably because I’m on the overweight/obese side.

(Side note: I do have PCOS, and it was actually my gynecologist who referred me to my psychiatrist.)

I just hope others had a better experience than I did. This moment doesn’t define me, but I want to highlight a deeper issue: aside from battling our own mental health struggles, we also face social stigma. What’s even more disappointing is that it came from a teacher—someone expected to guide and support others, not pass judgment.

In light of this election, I hope we choose leaders who are not tone-deaf to the realities many of us live with.

Another story, which might not be directly related to my situation: the person in front of me in line was also a PWD, and she was made to leave the line too. Out of frustration, she shouted, “Sino may kilala dito?! Sigurado ba kayong PWD ‘to?” That public confrontation forced a bystander to reveal that she had lost her eyesight due to diabetic retinopathy.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text describes a person with diabetic retinopathy being publicly confronted and forced to disclose her condition, showing direct discrimination (Experienced). The author also notes awareness of social

input text:
So much judgement with diabetes.  I am just wondering if I am the only one that feels this way..being diabetic is enough on its own. Maybe it causes you a lot of stress or maybe it doesn’t. Maybe it’s genetic, maybe it isn’t. Etc etc. But what drives me f*cking crazy is the judgement that I feel when people know my diagnosis! If someone knows that someone has thyroid disease or heart issues or Lupus or arthritis…no judgement there. But heaven help us if you’re a T2D! AND how it’s on SO many things as a warning (some legit I’m sure!) but I’m waiting for warnings to come out for simple things like “don’t breath air if you’re diabetic” or a questionnaire before you’re able to go to the movies or the mall- because you know- diabetes. 

I hate this disease and I feel for all who have to do deal with it- and for those that love and care for those that do. I just wish it wasn’t such a stigma. If you know or love someone dealing with this disease- please don’t make them feel bad about it. Treat them like a normal person- like someone who has a skin condition or a GI condition- not as someone who might implode at any second or as someone who did everything wrong to be where they are now. I’m sure there is a cure for this disease, and I hope someday soon it will be made available!

reasoning: The text describes feeling judged and stigmatized for having diabetes, especially T2D, compared to other conditions. The author anticipates and perceives negative

input text: ‘Betes beat me down today and peers have something to say. Long Rant: Even after changing my infusion site upon waking up with a 400, ithe BG refused to budge until coming home from work . I literally took insulin all day and ate very little. My emotions, mental and physical state are being pushed to the max and this stupid disease didn’t think I got enough burden already so it jumped on the band wagon to join the assault.  I’m dealing with more BS currently and to even share with colleagues that my disease is a  constant struggle is silent due to past criticism from the ignorance and underwhelming empathy being the constant. So I just suffer quietly as the judgement of my peers are muted but like an elephant in the room . I can only imagine the conversation-“Yes,  I came in 10 mins late again but I woke up with a severe high blood sugar and felt nauseous.” Them “oh…you gotta take better care of yourself!!” Only for me to rebuttal “I care a lot about my health and the work I put forth at this job is extensive as I give back 10 fold!” I worked 2 hours extra this week as an example to show my dedication to perform while others ppls standards are to give less time at work while chatting it up and not being a team player or change their own terrible shortcomings. It hurts to hear about them talking criticism behind my back and insulting my self worth when I put forth so much more than them but it’s shadowed by the misunderstood disease with me being tardy or taking my sick time. They have no care for understanding what I deal with constantly with this illness to be able to comment on to begin with but believe they are correct with the assumption of my problem being a time management  issue instead of a real legitimate disease causing my time problems at work. The emotional depth of understanding others struggles is so shallow it’s not worth teaching them my lifelong burden.  And this is a health related work place you’d think they have a little sympathy at least. Nope they don’t care and T1D doesn’t care about how much effort is put forth to control it either. It’s always a step ahead of me and it will win again (the minute I pay happy attention to something like smelling the flowers) causing me to suffer while making me look bad with the perception that I’m just a mindless fool with no work ethic. Easy to-never acknowledged me for actually going above ; beyond when at work all the time. Dedicated to working hard while quietly treating my symptoms without annoying others with my complex life as if they could even try to imagine it. The comfy life they live with the little road blocks that pop up but create a dramatic episode of “why me” when it’s not more than just adulting. Stop complaining about a minimal inconvenience to your perfect life.  This too shall pass. But me no-I got adulting and ‘betes at age 4 and now for 35 years has brought wisdom but also more and more other health disparities as I aged. It’s just getting harder. And more effort put forth by me only to be misunderstood and judged by the ignorant . I hate feeling misunderstood ontop of a blood sugar stagnating  persistent with a high or low. So my mood today is so over it and I’m just sad.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text describes direct criticism and judgment from peers (Experienced), awareness of negative assumptions (Perceived), fear of further criticism (Anticipated),

input text:
I was diagnosed with type 2 yesterday Just like the title says. I was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes yesterday. It's my own fault. I'm only 22 years old.  

I was warned 6 months ago that my sugar was a little high and I should change my lifestyle before it becomes permanent. I ignored the problem and was in denial about how bad it was and now it's permanent. 

I feel so stupid. I struggle a lot with my mental health (don't need advice about that, I see a psychologist and psychiatrist regularly and am on anti-depressants/anti-anxiety meds) and often cope by emotional eating. 

I know my life isn't over and that with a healthy life style and meds it isn't a debilitating illness. However, I am so emotionally destraught. The stigma of the disease makes me feel so worthless, like I am even a bigger burden than I was before...

How did you cope with it? I know I will get over it with time and i have a good support system around me, but what are like immediate thoughts that helped you?

(I have to go back to the hospital for a blood draw, so they can check wether it's Type 1 because my sugar jumped to 265 in 6 months, but the endocrinologist said it's just to rule it out.  
They put me on uni diamicron 60mg and metformine (start with 425mg once a day and build up) and i feel really nauseous and dizzy but i don't know wether its because of the meds or because i'm anxious. I mean this is like the first day I'm taking them so it's probably just nerves;) 

Thanks already for reading this ramble and I wish you a nice day!

English is not my first language so I'm sorry for any grammatical and spelling errors
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The writer expresses self-blame ("It's my own fault," "I feel so stupid") and feelings of worthlessness due to diabetes, indicating internalized stigma. They also mention

input text: Just Diagnosed with Type 2 Diabetes Hi everyone. 32 M here. I just got diagnosed with Type 2 Diabetes (**260 post-prandial** and **7.2% HbA1c**) . I don't know the implications of this. I am right now distraught and trying not to cry my eyes out. Most of my knowledge about diabetes is from the stigma around me that it is a shitty condition to have.  I have been Gyming since the last 1.5 years and I don't indulge in much junk food for that reason. Its mostly boiled eggs, chicken, lentils and occasional McD and cake or biscuits on weekends. I understand I have to give up a lot of food and thats okay, I would miss my sugar with coffee though. But 21 days without it and I am sure I will make it a habit. I have no problem giving up things that I liked to eat as cheat meal or something as long as I can have some eggs and chicken, boiled or grilled. My real problem is how everyone will perceive me  because of this. My partner is right now on a much needed and deserved trip with her friends. she is calling me to ask what the reports said but I don't want to inform her this right away and maybe spoil her trip with this weighing down on her. But what does this mean for us? Is our future jeopardized because of this? I don't want to be a burden on her. My thoughts are spiraling and I am thinking of all negative things.

input text:
The emotional side of this disease I'd like to talk a bit about the emotional side of this disease especially amongst the newly diagnosed. I feel a lot of stigma because I'm not getting much understanding on the diet from the people around me. Don't understand how suddenly someone can become gluten intolerant. I'm at a loss myself frankly. 

I haven't been loyal to the diet, I've been at it for 4 months now and I feel so... Not despairing exactly it's not that just helpless? I keep making all these mistakes and I feel like I'm never going to be able to entirely avoid all of it. 

My dad struggled with binge eating disorder and frankly even if I don't have the full-fledged disorder I think I have aspects of it. Partial if you will. And especially when there's an emotional trigger I have a really hard time not reaching for baked goods in particular. 

I know I shouldn't but I can't always bake the gluten-free versions whenever I want to and so I succumb to it. 

And I feel so bad about myself after I'm literally beating myself up harder than anyone on this thread possibly could. I know I could get cancer I know I could get malnutrition all of it. I'm starting to think I might need therapy in order to really avoid gluten. 

When my dad was newly diagnosed with diabetes his doctor told him either you listen to me and what I tell you and you die in 10 years or you don't listen to me and you die in five. Harsh I know but it did get him to take the diagnosis seriously. I'm not saying I need that precisely but maybe I need some version of a boot camp instructor or something. 

I don't really know anyone else with the disease that can be that person to me. I don't really know where I'm going with this just sending it out into the universe. I also just want to say that for the newly diagnosed people I completely understand what you're going through. Please don't feel guilty for having a weak moment and consuming gluten. For those who have been gluten free their entire lives you simply have more practice. I know the new people will get the hang of it eventually but when you've been able to largely consume as you please your entire life this is really difficult. 

Especially because I'm lactose intolerant as well this is frustrating but with lactose intolerance generally there are some things that you can eat and also there's lactose pills. Not so with this.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author expresses feeling stigma due to lack of understanding from others, self-blame for dietary mistakes, and emotional distress. They also mention compounded challenges from both diabetes

input text:
Kinda hating on the weight loss.  Back in 2016 I was diagnosed with diabetes. My A1C was 10.5. After trying several meds, I was given bydureon, later bydureon bcise. 
Fast forward to April 2023. New Insurance. Needed refills. They decided since my A1C was 5.6 that I did not need this medication (they didn’t pay for it) or anything similar. Told me to take metformin, which was on my record that I could not take it. I’m not sure if the insurance was that bad, or my doctor just sucked at filing things. I ran out of the bydureon in July 2023.  My doctor put me on a pill. By October my A1C went up to 7.7. Then January 2024 it was 9.5.  The insurance finally approved Ozempic. 
I never lost weight during the first 5 years on bydureon. I made changes of my own in about 2021 that resulted in me losing 50 lbs over the next 2 years. Yay me, right??  Since being on the Ozempic, I’ve lost a little less than 30 lbs.  People are now noticing, which is fine. However when I say that I have done it by cutting calories, and chasing around 4 of my grandkids that I got custody of last November, when they find out I’m on Ozempic and they are like oh, that’s why, and I just wanna hit them upside the head.  I would honestly prefer people not to notice or not say anything if they do notice. 
I didn’t realize there was the whole stigma against Ozempic until I came here. (Reddit) So of course that is all fueled into my brain now too.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author perceives others attributing their weight loss solely to Ozempic, dismissing their efforts, and expresses frustration and a desire for

input text:
I’m trying to fight fat bias one medical professional at a time and I need help / RANT CW: fatphobia, nurses saying bs fat bias related things, paternalistic treatment of fat people, multiple mentions of **** and ***** epidemic 

Tl;dr I’m arguing with a medical professional and need help finding research that proves that higher weight is not definitely correlated to worse health outcomes on an individual level, that there is no safe and effective weight loss intervention, especially for people who are very fat and have been forever/for a long time, that hearing “you should lose weight” at doctor’s appointment leads to DE or even ED, and how it’s not as simple as calories in calories out, I would appreciate any primary sources, whatever you have lying around or, if you want to look for some I would appreciate it even more (but I’m mostly asking for something you folks might already used or something, I plan to look myself and I don’t want to feel like I’m delegating my crusade on redditors hehe).

Quick context: I live in Poland and while I know a lot about fat bias and stigma in medical settings from the show among other things, I know next to nothing how the big picture of it looks here in my country.
I also follow some sci-comm adjacent accounts of Polish doctors, nurses and EMTs, all of them implicitly or explicitly left leaning. 

And yesterday I first saw that one of those people, a male nurse, shared in his story an Instagram post from national nurses and midwives association saying that the **** is so bad and it kills people yadda yadda so we are promoting a prophylactic health initiative which is… taking the stairs. 
I immediately wrote that yeah, those 5 kcal per flight of stairs will DEFINITELY make a difference. 
Then I saw next slide in his stories and I wanted to break something.

You can see the translation of the post below (it’s really bad so be advised):

“I'm going to address a difficult topic today. I've been observing my patients for several years and I can safely say that the Polish society is becoming increasingly ****. The heaviest patient I had weighed over 200kg.
I would also like to make it clear right away that **** is a disease that many people supposedly see, but little is done about it because we often avoid this topic not to offend someone, or we are afraid that it will be taken wrongly.
Respect is the most important thing. It's really important for us to feel good in our own body, however ***** is a complicated disease, that needs to be treated, not accepted.
You know, it's not about looks, I'm pushing them far, far away - it's about health, and even life because **** can lead to death.
9 million adults suffer from ***** in Poland. It’s caused by the fact that the amount of energy taken in is greater than that spent. Fat tissue that accumulates in huge amounts begins to wreak havoc. Adipocytes, i.e. fat cells not only accumulate energy but also produce a certain substance - Adipokine. It causes the development of Insulin resistance, and it leads to the development of many diseases that coexist with **** such as t2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, high blood pressure, or stroke. There are almost 200 of these diseases! **** has no ability to cure itself, that's why prevention, talking about it and treatment are so important - especially since the number of **** children is increasing dramatically, and the data says that Polish children gain weight the fastest in Europe.

Aside from various illnesses caused by ****, it also affects daily life. Poor stamina, hate, stigmatization, lead to self isolating and depression. Additionally, **** often means more complicated surgeries, worse wound healing, bigger chance for bedsores.
The longer *** lasts, the more complications arise, and the harder it is to treat the disease. Unfortunately with ***** it is not enough to "eat less and move more", here you need the full support of a dietitian, often a psychologist and treatment of diseases that have already developed.
There's no place to stigmatize people, because there's a story behind each of us. Let's not judge. Let's appreciate!” 

And if that wasn’t FUCK ALL THE WAY OFF content, he posted his before and after(!!!!) photos in which you can see a fat teenager and a fit adult man, and it has a caption that says:
“I know how it feels when you have too many kilograms and people call you <slur> and I know this isn’t motivating.
However I also know how much the support of close ones means, and that with that support you can accomplish everything. I also know that saying “I’m fine with my weight” is not true, that people who say that lie to themselves. <redacted because it’s extremely bad> it’s worth it to change the weight to be healthy” 

FUCK
ALL
THE
WAY
OFF

So I wrote him that it’s sad to see so much fatphobia and half truths and oversimplifications from a nurse, and I want hard data to made this into a teaching moment. 

If you can’t or don’t want to help you’re welcome to rant with me too.
I also truly hope I didn’t leave anything triggering in the text because the last thing I would want to do is to have mu crusade hurting someone.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text describes and critiques pervasive fatphobia and medical bias, including linking higher weight to type 2 diabetes and negative health outcomes. It highlights both

input text:
India must declare T1D as disability 

I was a cadet at Rashtriya Indian Military College (RIMC), Dehradun, living my dream of serving the nation. But that dream was cut short—not because I lacked the skill or determination, but because I was deemed "unfit" due to Type 1 Diabetes (T1D). It wasn’t a choice, nor something I could have prevented. Yet, I had to leave behind an institution I loved, simply because my condition wasn’t recognized for what it truly is—a lifelong medical challenge.

And I am not alone. Thousands of Type 1 Diabetics in India face the same struggle every day. Denied opportunities. Discriminated against. Overlooked.

The Reality of Living with T1D

Imagine having to constantly monitor your blood sugar, count every single carbohydrate you eat, take multiple insulin injections a day, and wake up at night to make sure you don’t slip into a coma. Now, imagine doing all this while also trying to focus on studies, a job, or even just living a normal life.

It’s exhausting, expensive, and unpredictable. A slight miscalculation can lead to dangerous lows (hypoglycemia) or life-threatening highs (ketoacidosis). Unlike Type 2 Diabetes, which can often be managed with lifestyle changes, T1D has no cure, no pause, no break. It’s there every single second, demanding attention.

The U.S. and U.K. Already Recognize T1D as a Disability—Why Not India?

In the U.S., T1D is protected under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), ensuring that students and employees get necessary accommodations. Financial assistance is available for medical supplies like insulin pumps and continuous glucose monitors.

In the U.K., the government provides disability benefits to help T1D patients cover the high cost of insulin and medical equipment. They understand that managing T1D is more than just a personal struggle—it’s a public health issue that needs support.

So why is India ignoring the reality of thousands of its own people?

Why India Must Act Now

1. Equal Opportunities in Education ; Jobs

Students with T1D face discrimination in schools and universities. Many institutions don’t allow medical accommodations like carrying glucose or checking blood sugar during exams. Some companies hesitate to hire T1D patients, assuming they’re a liability.

Recognizing T1D as a disability will ensure equal access to education and employment without bias.



2. Financial Support for Insulin ; Medical Supplies

T1D management is expensive in India. Insulin, glucose monitors, and emergency hospital visits drain families financially.

If recognized as a disability, patients could get government subsidies, insurance coverage, and financial aid, making treatment accessible to everyone.



3. Disability Benefits ; Social Security

Under the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPWD) Act, T1D patients could qualify for tax benefits, travel concessions, and even disability pensions—just like in the U.S. and U.K.



4. Raising Awareness ; Fighting Stigma

Many still confuse T1D with Type 2 Diabetes, blaming patients for their condition. T1D isn’t caused by poor diet or lifestyle—it’s an autoimmune disease that anyone can get, even children.

Recognizing it as a disability will change public perception and promote acceptance, making it easier for T1D patients to live without shame or stigma.




Enough is Enough—It's Time for Change!

If the U.S. and U.K. can acknowledge the struggles of T1D patients and provide them with financial and social support, why should India lag behind?

This isn’t about sympathy—it’s about justice, rights, and equality. People with Type 1 Diabetes aren’t asking for special treatment. They’re asking for fair treatment.

We need to speak up, raise awareness, push for policy changes, and demand that India recognizes Type 1 Diabetes as a disability. Because no one should have to fight their country while already fighting a lifelong medical condition.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text describes direct discrimination (expulsion from RIMC, denied opportunities), awareness of negative societal views, fear of future exclusion

input text:
How We Talk About Bipolar Matters We see this phrasing often:  
“My Bipolar husband”  
“My Bipolar daughter”  
“My Bipolar friend”

It may seem harmless or descriptive, but this kind of language is **dehumanizing**. It reduces a whole person to a diagnosis and subtly implies that their identity is defined by their condition, or that they are a possession.

Would you say “my Arthritis cousin”?  
“My Diabetes wife”?  
“My Schizophrenia brother”?  
It sounds strange, right? Because we instinctively know that people are more than their medical conditions.

So why does it feel more acceptable with mental health diagnoses?

Here’s why it matters:

  - **It reinforces stigma** by making the diagnosis the defining trait  

  - **It erases the person’s individuality** and full humanity  

  - **It implies ownership or control**, especially in phrases like “my Bipolar”  

  - **It affects how others perceive and treat your loved one**, including how they see themselves  

  - **It contributes to emotional distancing**, especially when used in moments of frustration or anger  

Instead, try person-first language:  
“My husband, who lives with Bipolar Disorder”  
“My daughter, who was diagnosed with Bipolar”  
“My friend, who’s navigating Bipolar symptoms”

This small shift honors their identity and reminds us that Bipolar is something they experience, not something they are.

**Words have weight.** In mental health support spaces, the language we use can either build connection or deepen misunderstanding. Vents are welcome here, but it’s worth remembering that words said in anger often reveal deeper beliefs and those beliefs can shape how we treat the people we love.

Being intentional with language doesn’t mean censoring emotion. It means choosing words that reflect compassion, clarity, and respect, especially when things are hard.

 For more on respectful language in mental health:  
- [Carepatron – Recognizing and Avoiding Dehumanizing Language in   
- [APA – Fighting Stigma by Mental Health Providers Toward
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text highlights how referring to someone as "my Diabetes wife" is dehumanizing and stigmatizing, implying that such language reduces a person to their diagnosis. It discusses the negative impact

input text:
A strong why to stay off Zyn for good I've been on Zyn for five years and finally assembled a strong why to quit and stay quit. Here are all the ways Zyn takes rather than gives:

# 1. Cardiovascular Risks

* **Increased Blood Pressure ; Heart Rate** – Nicotine causes vasoconstriction, making your heart work harder. Elevated blood pressure is a big risk factor for later life disorders.
* **Higher Risk of Heart Disease** – Long-term nicotine use damages blood vessels and increases the risk of hypertension, heart attacks, and strokes.
* **Atherosclerosis (Artery Narrowing)** – Nicotine contributes to plaque buildup, which can lead to blocked arteries.

# 2. Brain ; Mental Health Impacts 

* **Addiction ; Dependence** – Nicotine rewires your brain, making it harder to feel pleasure without it.
* **Anxiety ; Stress** – While Zyn may feel like a stress reliever, nicotine withdrawal actually increases anxiety over time.
* **Brain Fog ; Poor Focus** – Nicotine messes with your brain’s dopamine system, making concentration harder without a hit.
* **Dopamine Depletion** – Over time, your natural motivation and reward system weaken, making everyday activities less enjoyable.

# 3. Oral ; Dental Health Issues 

* **Gum Recession ; Disease** – Nicotine reduces blood flow to the gums. Less blood flow = gums pulling back = eventual tooth problems. and dental work is EXPENSIVE.
* **Tooth Decay ; Cavities** – Zyn contain sweeteners, which can lead to tooth decay and cavities.
* **Mouth Sores ; Irritation** – The pouch material can cause chronic irritation, leading to sores, sensitivity, and even keratosis (pre-cancerous white patches).

# 4. Digestive Issues ; Stomach Problems 

* **Acid Reflux ; Heartburn** – Nicotine weakens the lower esophageal sphincter, increasing acid reflux and discomfort.
* **Nausea ; Stomach Pain** – Being a stimulant, nicotine can upset your stomach lining and cause nausea.
* **Potential Gut Microbiome Damage** – Nicotine can disrupt the balance of good bacteria in your gut, which can impact digestion and immune health.

# 5. Hormonal ; Metabolic Effects 

* **Increased Cortisol (Stress Hormone)** – Chronic nicotine use can lead to elevated stress levels, making you feel more anxious in the long run.
* **Blood Sugar Spikes ; Crashes** – Nicotine messes with insulin regulation, increasing the risk of type 2 diabetes.
* **Potential Fertility Issues** – Nicotine can reduce sperm quality in men and affect menstrual cycles in women.

# 6. Long-Term Health Concerns 

* **Higher Risk of Chronic Diseases** – Nicotine use is linked to **neurodegenerative disorders,** heart disease, and metabolic syndrome.
* **Weakened Immune System** – Chronic nicotine use may suppress immune function, making you more vulnerable to illnesses.
* **Sleep Disturbances** – Nicotine disrupts sleep patterns, making it harder to get deep, restorative sleep.

# 7. Financial ; Lifestyle Impact 

* **Wasted Money** – If you're dropping $200+ a month on Zyn, that's $2400+ a year. You could literally take a dream vacation anywhere with that saved. Or invest it. (Pro tip: use the 752 rule — weekly expense x 752 = how much you’d have in 10 years with 7% investment returns. $50/week = $37,600 in 10 years. Wild)
* **Loss of Control** – Being dependent on a substance means you’re not fully in control of your life and habits.
* **Social Stigma** – Many people see nicotine addiction as a bad habit, and quitting improves self-respect and confidence.

**Bottom line:** Zyn isn’t just a harmless little habit. It taxes multiple things at once: your body, your brain, your wallet, and your life more than you think. And the longer you stay, the harder it hurts your future self. Time to break free, and stay free.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text mentions that nicotine "messes with insulin regulation, increasing the risk of type 2 diabetes," and discusses "blood sugar spikes; crashes." It frames type 2 diabetes as a

input text:
Just got diagnosed and I can't stop crying I'm 31, physically fit, started off my pregnancy at a healthy weight, eat fairly clean already with a few treats here and there, but I got unlucky with my placenta and so here I am. This is my first pregnancy. I'm absolutely devastated with this diagnosis. My doctors have all been very supportive and positive and are all are saying that it may be likely that I won't have to change much about my diet especially since I'm already very careful with sugars though I may have to swap out the white rice and bread for whole grain which isn't a big deal. I already only have fruit as a dessert so I don't have a problem on that front.

My baby is even measuring right on track and my weight gain is right on track so it's frustrating to have this diagnosis.

Despite all this I just can't seem to stop crying. I know everything will be ok and I'll do everything I need to in order to keep my baby healthy, but I can't seem to reach the acceptance stage of grief. I'm trying to reconcile why I'm feeling so down. I mean I can step outside myself and see how ridiculous it is to cry over something like white rice. I think maybe it's a feeling of a loss of control over my own body perhaps? I think I also feel like people are judging me, thinking I could have done something to prevent it even though everyone in my life have been nothing but supportive. There's such a stigma around the word "diabetes" that I cringe at the word. It makes me feel like a leper.

I'm also worried that this diagnosis is going to make me resent my baby. I already am having a hard time feeling connected to him in the womb even though I feel his movements. It feels more like an alien inside me than a baby. But I'm also very excited to be a mother and am obsessing over everything about my little boy! I'm just feeling very confused about all the conflicting emotions.

Anyways, I just wanted to come here and vent to those who are in the same boat as me. So thanks for reading!

EDIT:

Just wanted to say what a great a community, I'm glad I posted because it allowed me to do some deeper introspection of myself and why I'm feeling certain feelings. Part of it is me projecting and part is the loss of control. I really appreciate the kind words and a lot of what you all have said has made me feel a bit better!

I just got my blood sugar monitor and I'm feeling a new sense of determination. I feel like being able to track things allows me to take back a little bit of control!
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author expresses internalized stigma by feeling devastated, ashamed, and comparing themselves to a "leper" due to the diabetes diagnosis. They also mention perceived stigma, fearing others

I 36F, unmarried and 36M separated. What are some complications I should expect? I 36F have been been in long term relationships but have never felt the urge to commit. My boyfriend 36M has been separated for last 18 months and we are dating for the last 5 months.

This without a doubt is the best relationship I have had especially since I had resigned to living a single life and had various situationships and FWBs after my break-up with my ltr in 2018.

I can see his face light up when we meet or connect on video chat, and I see myself changing and being in love more than what I have ever felt before.

He was going through a mutual divorce when we started dating but his ex-wife backed off and now wants to give it another try. He has, however, filed for a divorce independently.

I want to prepare myself for what is looking to be a very contentious divorce. Normally I would walk away from such a complex situation but with him, I want to give it a try. He deserves a partner who stands with him through all this and I want to do it for him.

Any advice here will be helpful.

ETA: They do not have kids together. He has raised himself up from poverty and earns extremely well. His wife is more educated than him but does not have a stable career.

He did mention that hell will have no fury like him if he is forced into an alimony. He is prepared to settle for loss of income for the time they were married (8 years) as per average income for her field. While I have no interest in his financial transactions, I wonder how to manage his emotions through this.

ETA 2: We are located in India where Divorce is considered as a major stigma. It was an arranged marriage and the first meeting to the wedding ceremony was in 3 months. They have lived separately more than together for 8 years of their marriage. He had ED due to diabetes. In my knowledge, they were sexually incompatible due to his health and her past trauma. We do enjoy a healthy sex life now after treatment and exercise and the works.

input text:
What to do if you're terrified of a diagnosis? So I'm a guy in my mid 20s and a couple of years back I was told I was pre-diabetic and needed to consider some dietary and exercise changes to prevent it from progressing. I have a diet heavily influenced by taste and texture aversions, a lot of super proccessed foods. It felt impossible to make the changes needed and I didn't know where to start, I saw a dietitian but they were about as helpful as a food pyramid poster considering trying to eat apple slices makes me panic.

I recently realized this is still an issue and it may have already progressed, but I need to deal with it even if it hasn't. I started trying to eat less sugary snacks, find less sugary bread for sandwiches, less stress eating. I'm trying to find a dietitian to consult who works with people on the autism spectrum or with extreme aversions who won't just tell me about the food groups. I'm still panicking though.

The most obvious step is to go to a doctor and have a blood test, but I haven't seen a general doctor in a couple of years now, and the thought of being diagnosed for real terrifies me. Once it's diagnosed it doesn't matter if I did turn things around one day, they'll never take it off and I'll have to deal with the consequences forever. I keep telling people around me that I'll do it when I've had some time to get it under control because I can't have the diagnosis. It doesn't sound good and I know it.

I just don't know what to do. Secretly I feel like there's nothing I can do, I feel like living comfortably and enjoying eating is already over. If I'm diagnosed then it's even more real, but trying to turn it around feels like I can't just enjoy life anymore and it feels futile anyway with how I normally eat. I have to obsess over every little thing now I'm the hopes that I'm still early enough to avoid the diagnosis and then keep doing it forever. It feels like a nightmare already and this is just me trying to avoid the stigma of actually having the diagnosis, because I know it exists and I wish it didn't. 

I'm sorry I sound so catastrophic. I know it isn't kind to people who are diagnosed with type 2 that I'm talking about it like a death sentence. Plenty of people manage their type 2, and still can enjoy food. I just feel like as someone who hasn't really had any vegetables or fruit in a decade that I wont be able to do it, and I don't know what to do now.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The writer expresses fear of being diagnosed due to anticipated stigma ("trying to avoid the stigma of actually having the diagnosis"), perceives societal stigma ("I know it
