input text:
Dad needs advice, please. Hello XXFitness,

I need help.  My daughter is 15 y.o. and approaching 240lbs.  I'm not exactly sure how much she weighs since I dare not ask.  My wife and I are happily married, 23 years, lived in the same home for 17 years and have two older boys in college (21 and 18).  We live in a nice middle class neighborhood and we make an above average income.

Everyone in the house is relatively fit. We're not all Tony Horton, but I've run 2 marathons and several other races, my wife's run a marathon and still likes to run 5k kinda stuff.  My two boys are pretty fit but aren't runners.

The issue is my daughter will not take any advice or help and gets extremely combative whenever either of us try to talk about eating right and exercising. We don't keep a ton of crap in the house, but she will not eat veggies regularly and will not start an exercise program. Her go-to meal is pasta, or bread.  If we say no to that, she says she's not hungry and vanishes upstairs to her room.

We are so concerned about her health but we are beside ourselves on what to do.  She's met with four different nutritionists over the past 3 or 4 years and all have tried to set up eating plans.  None of which she sticks to for more than a few weeks.  We try motivating her to workout and eat salads, but it never lasts.

On top of this, she insists on wearing these cut-off daisy duke jean shorts and shirts that are too small.  Almost like she's daring us to say something.

On the outside she seems happy (when we aren't harping on her) and she has friends and goes out to the mall and whatever.  She has sleep-overs, etc.  She seems well-adjusted, but I'm afraid deep down she hates being heavy and is just hiding it.  I have no idea what to do.

Thoughts?  What are we doing wrong? **Are** we doing this wrong?  I feel like just accepting her the way she is would be giving up and exposing her to the risk of heart disease, diabetes, etc.... Not to mention a lifetime of bad eating habits and the social stigma that, sadly, goes along with being overweight.....

**EDIT**: Wow, so many great replies, I'm truly moved!  Thank you all.  This is not something that I can reply overnight and say it all worked out, but many of you have given me and my wife some great ideas and so much support!  I can't say Thank You enough. It's a long road and she's a tough girl.  We'll get there.

To all those saying stop making comments to her about her weight - I NEVER make comments to her about her appearance - unless she tries to go out with her friends dressed inappropriately. I'd tell a thin daughter the same thing.  She's my daughter and she's not going outside with half her ass showing.  Not happening....

Also, she used to love soccer and was great at it.  Even on the travel/select team in our area. She got three concussions in two years and the doctor's recommended she take a break.  That was a year ago.  It is taking its toll on her. She doesn't want to play any other sport and will not "exercise" just to exercise...
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text links being overweight to the risk of diabetes and references "social stigma" associated with being overweight, anticipating negative judgment and discrimination. It also reflects intersection

input text:
m17, type2diabetic, college life 
to sum things up, diabetes has got 3 stages. 

1. remission
2. prediabetes
3. diabetes.

diagnosed late into last year already in stage 3. few months later, moving in to stage 2 now. used to be obese, but now kinda built cus i gymmed relentlessly (lost around 20 kilos). cut out sugars and most carbs. i’m on track to be off my medication, pretty much reversing the disease (though i understand i need to maintain my current lifestyle).

there’s a stigma around the disease that only fat (which i was) and old people (im not old i think) get the disease. i probably deserve some judgement from society for letting myself get to this point, but it still gnaws on me how this disease will affect my life moving forward as a young adult on his uni arc..

will i be made fun of? excluded from stuff like parties and groups for not having what is considered a conventional lifestyle that normal people live? will anyone even be willing to put up with my needs? i understand that personality and presentation are also important, but it only goes so far when it comes to tangible things that others have to deal with 

the academic year starts in 2 months, and these thoughts have been gnawing on me since summer started.. advice, blunt honesty, whatever. need something to work with, already feeling cynical before anything’s happened
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The writer expresses internalized stigma (self-blame, feeling deserving of judgment), anticipates future exclusion and ridicule, perceives societal stereotypes (

input text:
What was your reaction to being diagnosed? I’m upset and sad. I’m 27 weeks (35 y/o). I was diagnosed yesterday and felt devestated. I epically failed my 1hr glucose test with a 201. No 3 hr test, straight to GD school for me. 

My in-laws (who happen to be in town) keep giving me unsolicited advice (MIL had GD 35 years ago and FIL has Type 1 D) and repeatedly telling me “it’s going to be okay, it’s all very treatable.” I know it’s going be okay - my tears aren’t about that. I see their good intentions but give a girl a minute to feel the feels. 

I’m upset and teary because:
* I found out when a Kaiser customer service person called to schedule my “GD testing and treatment seminar”. No one from my OB office actually communicated the diagnosis directly to me. 
* When I started crying, the woman said “I don’t want you to be upset, it’s not good for the baby.” Oh, okay, guess I’m not allowed a human response? 
* ~~ I now have a 50% lifetime chance of developing type II diabetes- a risk that didn’t exist a week ago. ~~ Rephrasing for accuracy: I’m predisposed to T2D - a risk I wasn’t aware of a week ago
* How invasive this will be on my every day life. I’m sure you get used to it, but I’d just rather not? 
* This diagnosis activates dormant disordered/restrictive eating habits and thoughts (I’ve worked tirelessly to achieve body neutrality- maybe I am overweight and no one has told me?? Does everyone think I’m overweight? Maybe I should restrict my eating again? And down the spiral I go)
* I feel like a failure (I know it’s not my fault, but try telling that to my emotions and hormones)
* I’m angry at my placenta
* This was a surprise pregnancy that I just got excited about a couple weeks ago - couldn’t I have that feeling for just a few weeks? 
* (safe space on this one please) I felt resentment as I walked by the 20 week ultrasound on my fridge, then I felt guilt and anger at myself for feeling resentment - it’s not the baby’s fault.
* I feel grief over no longer having a “normalish” pregnancy 
* I feel deep anger at the societal stigma attached to diabetes because we live in a world that thinks thinness = the picture of health and beauty, ergo only unhealthy people get diabetes? (Obv not true but it’s a trope that I recognize and I’m trying to unpack) 
* I’m upset at the online GD training I had to take that actually had a slide on benefits (you get to build heathy habits! you could even lose weight!), the latter of which is what historically plunges me into restrictive eating. Kudos on the strength based approach but I’m too in my feels to see silverlinings yet. 
* I love carbs, and part of my healing from restrictive eating was embracing all foods and not obsessively tracking 
* Hormones

I’m sad and angry and could use some solidarity. What was your reaction to the diagnosis? When did you settle into acceptance? What helped you get there?

**Edit: Thank you to everyone who is responding. I feel the kindness, solidarity, and support. And I feel so much less alone. I’m sure this sub gets a lot of posts like this, and y’all showing so much love means a lot to me and my emotional rollercoaster**
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author expresses internalized stigma (feeling like a failure, shame, and guilt), perceived stigma (awareness of societal stereotypes about diabetes and weight), and

input text:
Musings three months in on Mounjaro and doctor check in - down 30+ lbs ( reactions from friends, whether to increase dose, etc) I've had a history of heart/diabetes (T2, though well controlled) issues over the years, along with big weight fluctuations. After some concerns and symptoms early this year, decided to take the bull by the horns and get aggressive to fix my health. Part of that was getting prescribed Mounjaro (I get insurance approval under the "well controlled diabetes" moniker, so I pay $25 a month for a refill). Started with 2.5mg and upped the dosage every time, just took my first 10MG dose earlier this week. I was 5'10, 260 right before Mounjaro in late March; I weighted in at 228 this AM. 5'10M with a good amount of muscle I am trying to retain.

1. **People are staring to ask if I am losing weight "intentionally."** Down 30+ pounds in three months, people are saying I look great - but some are asking in a concerning way (as if I have a medical problem). They ease up when I say it is intentional, and then go on to disucss how I am doing it. I get multiple comments on how my complexion has also improved, which I didn't expect.
2. **The reaction when I reveal I am on a GLP-1 (I don't disclose which drug typically, unless the person is a doctor or otherwise in the medical field) remains mixed at best - probably more negative than positive.** When people say what I am doing, I always say GLP1 + aggressive exercising + diet. Doctors say "That's great, Mounjaro is an awesome drug" - especially doctors that know my health history (generally my doctor friends). Other friends and colleagues still have a mixed reaction - some say "Oh - that really works? Is it natural?" when I reveal it. Others say they want to try it. It's a mix. I would say there still is a stigma out there in terms of these drugs despite the fact that using them is becoming increasingly common. I get my prescription filled at a hospital pharmacy - they always have a fridge full of Mounjaro/Ozempic/etc they are constantly dispersing, so these drugs must be more commonly used than understood. They seem to give it out like candy now, which I wholeheartedly approve now that all of the shortages seem to have gone away.
3. **People, and I, am questioning my final weight loss goal.** Many people have said "I don't need to lose more weight." I want to get to be under 200 lbs. However, I am a broad shouldered dude who used to play football - a lot of people who have known me for years think I should basically stop losing weight. Brought this up to my doctor today - he recommended I keep going to protect my heart long term, to keep diabetes and blood sugar levels basically in remission levels, etc. I already look different - going down another 30 lbs will make me look drastically different, which I think will create even more of reaction. I am going to keep going, I think. Aiming for 190-195 before I start thinking about maintenance.
4. **Doctor wants me to stay on 10MG.** He is happy with everything - all of my blood work has drastically improved in three months. A1C, which was around 5 when I started, is now in the mid 4s. All other metrics are also improving. Given the fast pace of weight loss, the doc said no more dosage increases. I am fine with that - so far, I feel full on even less food with 10MG, so it seems to be working the best out of all of the doses I have used so far.
5. **I still enjoy food!** The food noise stuff was never a big issue for me - I always just liked good food (restaurant food, cooking for myself, etc). I am eating almost no restaraunt food these days outside of lunches. All that said, I still like going to the grocery store, buying protein and veggies and occasional starches, trying new recipes, etc. I am still a foodie - but I just eat way less.
6. **Working out (cardio and weightlifting) is key.** I have increased my gym attendance to 4-5 days a week, with two or three weightlifting sessions, and two dedicated cardio days. My lifts have generally remained the same through this process (I can now comfortably rep 230-245 on a bench press for 4 or 5 times). But I kind of think cardio is a bit underrated when in Mounjaro - everyone correctly says that you need to lift weights to preserve muscle. But cardio, I find, seems to really accelerate weight and fat loss. The main benefit is that you aren't hungry after a tough cardio session like you may be normally. I do several classes at the gym (spin glass, interval treadmill class) along with occasional long stair master and elliptical sessions. I recommend pushing cardio as much as you can on this drug - it's also obviously good for the heart.
7. **For those that like alcohol, Mounjaro really helps you get a better relationship with drinking.** I am drinking way less - most alcohol drinks fill me up (including stuff like vodka+soda and wine). I get less of a dopamine rush when drinking with friends. I can still celebrate, but a big benefit of this drug is reducing stuff like drinking (which obviously has other downstream benefits, like better eating, better liver function, etc.). 
8. **Overall, I highly recommend the drug. IMO - anyone with any kind of health issue (even just high blood pressure, cholesterol, whatever) combined with obesity or even just being overweight on the scale should try it.** I now strongly believe that getting into a caloric deficit, working out and losing weight can address a whole host of health issues we all face. Mounjaro (and I presume the other drugs) make eating better and working out much easier and more effective. Many more people could benefit from being on these drugs. If 40% of Americans are obese, and even more are overweight, I don't see why over time, over half of Americans shouldn't be taking these drugs.

I'm the happiest I have been about my health for years. At some point, I will post some before and after photos. But for everyone on Mounjaro - keep it up. And for anyone looking into it - try it. It works!
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author notes mixed, often negative, reactions from others when disclosing GLP-1 use, referencing stigma around these drugs. They anticipate

input text: HAES is not helping my recovery First of all, I'd like to disclaim that this is coming from my personal experience only. I am not in any way saying that HAES cannot be helpful to people in ED recovery, it's just that it is not helping me. Background: I am anorexic and am currently a grad student and am incredibly fortunate that my university offers a weekly ED support session, which I attend. The thing is, the session is heavily HAES focused. While I fully support the notion that people come in all shapes and sizes and can be healthy while "overweight", I cannot comprehend the notion that even morbidly obese people cannot benefit from a certain amount of weight loss. I took quite a few courses in human biology and anthropology, and many of these point to medical research and studies that show how being significantly obese is harmful to health. It doesn't help that my mother is a medical doctor specializing in diabetes and does not believe in HAES, either. The fact that the group therapist in my university (as well as my personal ED therapist) constantly points to HAES makes me reluctant to process a lot of the other (very helpful) things that they say. In addition, it makes it impossible to bring up my fear of gaining to an "unacceptable" weight, since every time I bring up the issue, the response is always something along the lies of "Everyone has a different healthy weight, and you need to decide what to prioritize." It's even more concerning for me because prior to developing an eating disorder, I was in the "slightly overweight" category, and I certainly did not enjoy the bullying and stigma that came with it. I understand that my current low body weight is not doing me any favors in terms of health, but am terrified of gaining back to my original body size and having to deal with what I went through as a child again. I really can't address these concerns without people telling me that I need to accept even the possibility of being obese, it that's the "healthy weight" for me. I'm so frustrated about this because I really am trying to recover. This community is the only place I feel safe sharing these thought with, and I would love any advice you guys have on dealing with this issue.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text references past experiences of bullying and stigma due to being "slightly overweight," with a specific mention of the author's mother being a diabetes specialist who does

input text:
Confusion I started taking Mounjaro based on my primary doctor's recommendation for insulin resistance and being pre-diabetic . Over the course of a year, I gained over 50 pounds, even though I lowered my calories to 1600 and incorporated walking and dancing games into my routine, yet the weight wouldn’t come off. I also struggled with high testosterone levels, and my menstrual cycle wouldn’t come for months or even years. While I was initially skeptical about Mounjaro's benefits for PCOS—especially since many people use it for diabetes or weight loss—I've been on the injection for almost three months now. To my surprise, my periods have been consistent and like clockwork.

Even though I may be on this medication forever, my hard work is finally starting to pay off. I've gone down almost three pant sizes and lost 20 pounds in just under three months. I was so happy, I was on the verge of tears.

Some people may view it as an "easier way" because of the stigma or what they see on social media, but they don’t fully understand. If your body isn’t responding the way it should, and a medication like this helps, there should be no issue with using it. Everyone's journey is different, and what works for one person might be exactly what another needs to finally see progress.

Sorry for the rant, but I just had to get this off my chest.
label: Yes stigma

input text:
Approved for Zepbound, but scared to start. **About Me:**  
I’m a 37-year-old male, 6’1” tall, weighing 250 pounds. While I don’t have other weight-related conditions, my BMI is above 30, which qualifies me for weight-loss treatment. Diabetes runs in my family—both my father and brother have it, though they have higher BMIs than me.

**Weight History:**  
I’ve always been on the heavier side. My highest weight was around 275 pounds, but I did get into great shape in my early 20s, dropping to 190-200 pounds through consistent exercise and diet. I maintained that for about five years. After graduation, I stayed active and gained some weight, hovering around 215-220 pounds, which felt ideal for me despite the BMI charts.

Like many, life got busier—marriage, kids, and less time for exercise. For the past 10+ years, my weight has been relatively stable, ranging between 245-265 pounds. I’ve had periods of focus where I could diet and exercise for a few months, but it’s hard to sustain those habits long-term like I could in my 20s.

**Current Motivation:**  
Looking at my young kids (1 and 3), I realize I want to be healthier—for them and myself. I don’t want to be the dad in his 40s who isn’t fit enough to play with his kids. While I don’t feel extremely unfit, the family history of diabetes and my weight are concerning. I’ve been able to complete a few 5K races recently (not fast, but I finished!), and I occasionally do light resistance training and cardio, though I struggle to maintain consistency.

At my recent physical, I asked my doctor about weight-loss options and was referred to a clinic. They prescribed **Zepbound**, and thankfully, my insurance covers it at just $30 for a 28-day supply.

**Concerns About Starting Zepbound:**  
I’m excited but also nervous for several reasons:

1. **Side Effects:** I’m worried about potential severe side effects impacting my daily life. My wife works nights in a hospital, so I’m often taking care of two young kids alone.
2. **Health Risks (Pancreatitis ; Thyroid Cancer):** I know the risks are rare or unproven, but they still weigh on my mind. I try to remind myself that the long-term risks of staying obese likely outweigh these concerns.
3. **Enjoying Food:** I want to enjoy food in moderation and not feel like I’m only eating to meet a protein goal.
4. **Stigma:** I’m unsure how to handle potential stigma around taking Zepbound. I’m fine sharing this with close family, but I’m hesitant to discuss it with friends or coworkers if they comment on my weight.
5. **Long-Term Use:** I worry about needing this medication forever. My goal is to use Zepbound as a tool to retrain my brain to eat better and reestablish a consistent exercise routine. Seeing the number on the scale drop will motivate me to keep up with exercise. Ideally, I’d like to taper off the medication eventually. I know the risk of regaining weight is high, but I hope to build sustainable habits.

**Looking for Encouragement:**  
I’m reaching out to the Zepbound community for support. How did you overcome similar concerns? Any advice to ease my mind and get started would be greatly appreciated.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author expresses concern about potential stigma related to taking Zepbound, especially regarding how friends or coworkers might react to their weight-loss medication. This reflects both anticipated stigma (

input text:
Partner just started a month after I began! Soon, my mom will start! Excited for my partner to experience what I have! And looking forward to hopefully getting my mom started on this - at this point, she seems convinced based on my experience and the information I have given her about the studies, etc! All of us are starting obese to morbidly obese, and have struggled with weight for yeeeears.

I am so glad to have this tool which will hopefully mean both added quantity and added quality to the years I have with my partner, and my mom. My mom especially - she is at an age where it is even harder to lose weight, and we have a history of diabetes and Alzheimer's on her side, so hoping this might help to fend those off (since Alzheimer's has been linked to diabetes/blood sugar issues.)

I know that a lot of people, unfortunately and understandably, either cannot tell their family about their GLP1 use, or they have shared it and have sadly run into all kinds of judgement. I am really fortunate to be in a position where the people closest to me will, at best, find that me sharing my experience is a catalyst for them to get going on accessing this tool and changing their health, or, at worst, only share understandable and sincere concern about the safety and ask good faith questions with the intention to learn.

For those of you on the fence about sharing mostly due to stigma, maybe consider sharing this with people you trust, especially people who might also benefit from this medication. We shouldn't be telling anyone what to do with their bodies, of course. But I started this because my sister, who also struggles with weight, eventually shared with me that she was utilizing compounded semaglutide to help her. I might have eventually sought out a GLP1, but her sharing her experience with me was my catalyst - and now my experience is a catalyst for my partner, and these experiences are likely to be a catalyst for my mom. These meds are going to help us change the trajectory of our health, and even parts of our lives, like being more comfortable going places and traveling both because we are more comfortable in our bodies and also because it is easier to keep up without so much extra weight!
label: Yes stigma

Dating as a Diabetic Hey everyone!

Figured based on the responses to my last post that I would show another video I have been wanting to make for a while - it's about dating as a Diabetic:





I'm not sure how you guys feel, but in the past at least I've been feeling it as a daunting task to share my Diabetes during dates, especially for the fear of judgement from others.

I am dispelling some myths and stigmas, that I think are super important as a Diabetic to keep in mind - both in life, but especially when dating. Myths are as below:

**Myth number 1: You should keep Diabetes a secret**  
**Myth 2 - Make Diabetes More Serious Than It Is**  
**Myth number 3: Diabetics can’t do a lot of date activities**  
**Myth number 4: Diabetics can’t date “healthy people”**  
**Myth number 5: Diabetes gets in the way of sex**

as well as a bonus tip in the end on how I live life with Diabetes in order to get the maximum life value (as I see it, at least. It really changed everything for me).

I'm hoping to get your input, feedback as well as stories from your dating life to maybe help each other out in this aspect of life! Thanks for giving me the confidence from the last post in order to create and share this as well :D

We live in a society that's designed to make you sick (physically and mentally) Now at the age of 25, I've come to realize that we live in a society that is designed to make you sick, physically and mentally.

First off, at least here in the US, the average American diet is disgusting and awful. No wonder we have such a high rate of obesity and diabetes. For people who are financially struggling, fast food is actually a cheaper alternative. And for people on a limited budget, buying unhealthy junk food at the store is a cheaper alternative than buying healthy food.

I look at stores differently now. We are all guinea pigs that are taking part in a corporate experiment. Our food is packed with sugar, artificial flavors, and toxic chemicals. And the sad part is, many people aren't even educated about this. They simply eat whatever is available to them, and don't question what's in their food. And to make matters worse, people look at individuals who do monitor what's in their food, as if they're too picky or weird for doing so. Eating healthy actually comes with a negative sense of stigma.

The vegan diet is a complete agenda, and is not a sustainable diet. Us humans need animal protein in our diet. But yet again, even our meat is tainted. Literally, everything at the store is tainted, even the fruits and vegetables, all thanks to GMO's. It's really hard to say where our food comes from these days. Every time you buy something at the store and put it into your body, you're basically taking a risk. But then again, what are your options? Starve? And that doesn't even include our shampoos, tooth paste, hand soaps, bars of soap, cologne, mouthwash, etc. Literally, chemicals upon chemicals. This is why cancer rates are off the charts, and why BigPharma is making a killing from cancer treatments. BigPharma is creating illnesses to cure illnesses. It's all one big money game, so that way BigPharma can line it's pockets. Make no mistake about it, BigPharma is a terrorist organization. They committed and currently are committing crimes against humanity.

Don't even get me started on how evil vaccines are. And if you are one of those pro-vaxxers...trust me, you're not gonna sell me. I'm not saying that all vaccines are bad. But there are many vaccines that are. Immune-enhancing vaccines are all part of an agenda. Humans DO NOT need them. But what's even worse, is the fact that the government pushes these vaccines on children, and that parents are deemed neglectful if they don't vaccinate their children. Like I said, we're all guinea pigs and this is all part of a broader agenda to experiment on the masses. We are all part of a 'corporate experiment'.

And then there's the mental aspect of it all. We live in an era where social media, smart phones, cable television, and video games have taken over. People are glued to screens nowadays. Many people aren't even acknowledging the world around them. Many people are in a trance. Social networks like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram have hypnotized people. They're not even aware of it. Our society is being psychologically programmed and damaged. This is why the rates of depression, anxiety, suicide, and addiction are so high. And yet again...what does this result in? Another way for BigPharma to line it's pockets, pushing antidepressants and other experimental medications.

I could go on and on. Our society is so messed up right now.

I’m so tired of people blaming him My dad died from diabetic keto acidosis. He died in his sleep and it was very unexpected. I have a very small support system and losing him has been devastating. 

People have not been very empathetic and often comment about how he didn’t eat well/he didn’t take care of himself. One of my aunts said that he must have had too many beers before bed and that he loved pizza. It’s just mind blowing considering how much he tried. He always checked his blood sugar and took his medicine. He went to all of his appointments. He knew how different foods would impact his glucose levels. He wasn’t perfect but he tried. I just can’t put into words how sick I am of people telling me he didn’t “take care of his diabetes.”

Edit to add: Thanks so much, all you kind strangers. I appreciate the validation and support. Diabetes is a progressive disease that can kill even the most dedicated people. The shame and stigma around this medical condition is an issue. He was more than a diabetic he was a happy, resilient, hardworking, loving man. Grief is so tough and having a group to go to when I need to feel not so alone is great. Thank you all.

input text:
Is it 'Cheating' to Use Medication for Weight Loss? Breaking Down the Stigma, the Science, and the Reality

The Accusation: “You’re Just Taking the Easy Way Out”

If you’ve started or are considering weight loss medication—like Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, Zepbound, or others—you’ve probably heard this line, directly or indirectly:

> “Isn’t that just cheating?”
“Why not just eat less and move more?”
“I did it the hard way—you should too.”

The idea that using a medically-supported tool for weight loss is somehow “cheating” is rooted in stigma, misinformation, and outdated thinking about obesity. Let’s break it down:

1. Obesity Is Not Just a Willpower Problem—It’s a Disease

The American Medical Association, World Health Organization, and CDC all recognize obesity as a chronic, relapsing, multifactorial disease. It’s driven by:

Genetics (up to 70% heritability in some studies)

Hormonal dysregulation (e.g. leptin, insulin, GLP-1)

Neurological signaling affecting appetite, reward, and satiety

Environmental triggers (food availability, stress, sleep deprivation)

So when someone says, “Just try harder,” it’s a bit like telling someone with Type 1 diabetes to “just try harder to make insulin.”

2. How Weight Loss Meds Actually Work (It’s Not Magic)

GLP-1 receptor agonists (like semaglutide and tirzepatide) mimic natural hormones involved in satiety and insulin regulation. They:

Slow gastric emptying (you feel full longer)

Increase insulin sensitivity

Reduce food noise and cravings (especially for high-calorie foods)

Act on the hypothalamus, regulating hunger signals

These medications don't override willpower—they support the biological systems that many people with obesity have working against them.

3. If It Were Easy, Everyone Would Be Doing It

Let’s not sugarcoat it—these meds come with challenges:

Side effects: nausea, vomiting, fatigue, constipation

Strict dietary changes: high-fat meals often cause discomfort

Ongoing costs: $800–$1,200/month without insurance

Plateaus and weight regain: common after stopping

Emotional work: dealing with identity shifts, food relationships, and stigma

This is not the "easy way out." It's the clinically supported, effective, but still demanding way through.

4. The Double Standard: “Hard Work” Is OK—Until It’s Medical

Nobody says a person with depression is “cheating” by taking antidepressants.
Nobody tells someone with high blood pressure to “just stop stressing.”

And yet, with obesity, medical treatment is often labeled “cheating” while unsustainable crash diets, surgery, and dangerous exercise plans are weirdly praised for being “hardcore.”

Why? Because society still moralizes weight, assigning shame to bodies that don’t conform—and assuming that struggle is the price of change.

5. So Is It Cheating?

Let’s redefine the question.

> Is using a safe, effective, and doctor-prescribed treatment for a chronic disease “cheating”?
No. It's treatment.

Choosing medication doesn't mean you’ve failed. It means:

You’re taking your health seriously.

You’re using tools grounded in evidence.

You’re acknowledging that this isn’t just about willpower—because it never was.

Final Thought: You’re Not Alone, and You’re Not Cheating

If you’re on weight loss meds, you’re still doing the work. You’re unlearning old habits, navigating side effects, facing judgment, and staying committed to your health. That’s not weakness—it’s strength with support.

Don’t let stigma shame you out of progress.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses stigma around using medication for weight loss, referencing diabetes (e.g., GLP-1 agonists, insulin, Type

input text:
Health judgment in this sub I stopped following DD due to the constant violation of the children's privacy both at home and online. I also think the amount of food they reportedly eat is excessive. 

However, I find this sub problematic too. I’m very bothered by the amount of judgment that is thrown around here about illness, diet, weight, diagnoses, etc. Not because I care one whit about Alicia, but because I know discourse like this increases stigma towards people who are disabled or chronically ill. 

When you say- “she’s giving all her kids diabetes (or heart disease, etc.)” it contributes to the idea that everyone who has diabetes brought it on themselves, and that diet is the sole cause. 

When you point out the weight of the kids, you assume it’s all from the food they are served. The fact that there are differences among the kids themselves show it’s not just about diet- genetics, meds, activity all play a part. 

When you say her kids can’t have certain diagnoses or they wouldn’t eat the way they do, you contribute to a culture that assumes all illness looks one way, and if it doesn’t, people are faking. 

I imagine people are going to reply with things like “are you saying McDonalds every meal is good for you?” 

No! I’m saying I wish this sub would acknowledge that: 

1. Not all healthy people look the same. 
2. Illness is caused by interactions of genes, environment ; experiences. People can control some things but not all. 
3. Illness is not a moral failing. 
4. All food is chemicals. Anything can be part of a balanced diet. 
5. Constantly talking about how food is killing the DD increases judgement towards sick people by spreading the idea that you can draw a straight line from a slice of pizza to a high blood pressure diagnosis.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses how discourse in the community increases stigma toward people with diabetes by promoting the idea that diabetes is solely caused by diet and is a moral failing. It highlights awareness

input text:
So guess what: I’m oficially diagnosed as a type 1 diabetic! So here’s my symtoms story:

December last year, I started to feel a ridiculous amount of thirst that no amount of water could quench, then followed by other more odd symptoms such as dry mouth that seemed like the Saharra dessert decided to relocate and they chose my mouth as the perfect new place, I also looost lots of my damn hair.  My FBS came back around 280 and my a1c was like 13% 

And hey the 70 poundweight loss that I enjoyed for quite some time cause it felt like god or some sort of white daddy from above heard and finally grant my wish: be able to eat like a maniac and never gained an ounce of weight but rather lose. I let this things went on for 9 months — ik so damn stupid, so don’t be like me, kids. God I sound like the mothers from our neigborhood whenever they hear my story.

My T1 was confirmed by a C-Peptide test; it came back as 0.24 and the normal ref range was between 1:10 - 4:40 ng/ml. My doctor said that my result and symptoms clearly speak T1. Tho this was a fasted test, and I was currently injecting insulin when the test was conducted so I’m still not convinced or skeptical by it. Would talk to my doc abt those antibody test.

So yeah, I don’t know what my life would be like for the next years (hopefully I’ll be able to make it even at 60 or 70 as from what I read online T1s lifespan are reduced, tho I believe that I have my own journey.) I am trying to be optimistic about having a cure it the next XXX years lol. Despite everything, I still feel privileged that that I got to have this stupid disease in an era where technology is fucking amazing for diabetics and it gave us the chance to live way longer than people who had this sorry disease at a time where these things were non-existent.  

I wanna slap my neighbors and relatives that say I got diabetes cause I ate crap. And those who forces their idea and tell a ton of unsolicited advice; I’ve got a neighbor who told me that I should try the insulin plant. In my head, I was like Banting would stand from his grave just to flip you off for this shitty advice. Being a tool used by moms for their kids why they should always eat their greens and why they shouldn’t eat candies was so damn annoying or else they might end up like the sorry me. 

I realized that T1 is being sorrounded by lots of  stigmas from type 2, and to say that I am tired of explaining the difference between T1 and T2 to these idiots is an understatement. I don’t expect everyone to know what T1 is and how it works, but at least they can be kind with their words, be more encouraging, and understanding.

But, I firmly believe that regardless of type, no one should be blamed for their diabetes esp if they don’t buy our meds. To everyone who’s experiencing this, T1 or T2, you didn’t deserve to get diabetes. 

On the brigther side, tho in a hard way, my T1D taught me the importance of empathy and kindness. Oh hey, I don’t have to attend PE class lol sorry but imma play my diabeetus card. 

I am hoping that college would be kind to me; I hope that whenever ppl see me injecting my insulin they wouldn’t find me freaky or assume that I am doing drugs.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text describes direct stigma from neighbors/relatives (Experienced), awareness of negative stereotypes (Perceived), fear of being judged in

input text:
Fentanyl, Alcohol, and Tobacco In many of my previous articles and specifically the one titled “***Dysfunctional Autonomic Thinking Patterns*** (Do we have free will)”, I make the case that many people are making use of a very prehistoric part of our brain (brain stem, the limbic system, and the amygdala) to make decisions. The Limbic and Reptilian brain’s are excellent at making rapid, binary (yes/no) decisions when facing clear and present danger. These are very sub-optimal when trying to solve complex problems. ***All the world’s simple problems have been solved***, but a multitude of complex problems still need solving using the Neocortex or prefrontal cortex. Our evolutionary biology has allowed the Limbic and Reptilian brains, the 1st crack at problems when there is a fear response involved. Unfortunately, most our big problems are rather scary, so we need to try override our autonomic responses if we are going to make progress.



The prefrontal cortex must be used to solve complex problems, and even then we must still proceed with caution. Unintended consequences, are always possible and the magnitude of which can easily exceed the original problem many times over.  I will list a few examples below.

* The British government, concerned about the number of venomous cobras in Delhi, offered a ***bounty for every dead cobra***. Initially, this was a successful strategy; large numbers of snakes were killed for the reward. Eventually, however, ***people began to*** ***breed cobras for the income***.
* During the Great Leap Forward in China, Mao Zedong launched a ***campaign to eliminate sparrows,*** believing they were a threat to crops. This campaign, aimed to increase agricultural production, but ultimately harmed the ecosystem and contributed to a ***devastating famine***. The removal of sparrows disrupted the natural balance, allowing other pests, like locusts, to flourish, further damaging crops.
* One can only image what will happen if the UK government gets their way to block out the sun?

**Alcohol and Tobacco**

I haven’t consumed alcohol for 20 years and have never smoked. I in no way endorse anyone taking up their consumption as there are so many negative consequences. To date, I don’t believe that there has been a substance that has caused more deaths, destroyed more lives and caused more sub-optimal decision making than alcohol. Due to the devastating effects of alcohol, the US banned it from 1920 to 1933. While the law aimed to reduce crime and other social issues, it instead led to a rise in organized crime, the likes of which, has still not been brought under control. The ban had to be repealed and governments now rely on ever increasing taxes to make it less and less affordable. Alcohol consumption reduced 4% (2010 -2019) in spite of a population increase of 11%. Taxes have similarly been used to bring tobacco sales down 46% between (1990 -2019) despite a population growth of 47%.

This is where the prefrontal cortex comes in. ***Alcohol and Tobacco are evil, but could they be the lesser of the two evils?***

Importantly, for a large portion of the world’s population, life is unbearably hard. Alcohol takes the edge off misery for a while and tobacco similarly relieves anxiety for a time.

The hypothesis I’m putting forward is this: isn’t it worth considering whether the huge increase in Fentanyl and other drugs may be fueled by the absorbent cost and social stigma that has been placed on these “age old” drugs. Fentanyl deaths doubled from 2019 to 2022. In 2021 alone, over 107,000 Americans died of overdoses

Couldn’t alcohol and tobacco be made prescription drugs to allow people limited quantities at reasonable prices? It is not that these drugs are not already available and would introduce an unknown risk. The issue is, pricing is keeping it out of the hands of those that need it most.

There is a direct correlation between being poor and being vulnerable. The vulnerable need medication to get them through the day. It’s clear that Fentanyl isn’t the best for these susceptible individual or the broader society. We have never seen large groups of paralytic alcoholics filling our cities to the like of which Fentanyl is doing. Smokers can be smelly and anti social with their smoke, but their behaviour generally is better with tobacco, than without.

All drugs, especially those taken long term have negative consequences. Statins (allegedly to reduce cardiac arrests), are the most prescribed medication on the market. Long-term use of statins, can lead to several side effects, including muscle problems, liver damage, and increased risk of type 2 diabetes. New evidence shows that benefits have historically been way over stated.

***If we stop looking for perfect solutions;***

* ***we can start trying to move things incrementally in the right direction,***
* ***we will be so much better off than maintaining the status quo,***
* ***we can stop throwing rhetoric at one another and make the world a better place***

There are no one off answers, but rather a continual refinement of ideas.

Carl Jung famously said, “thinking is hard, that’s why we judge”. Let’s stop judging and do what is hard, let’s all aim upwards and make incremental improvements and break out of the confines of our rigid thinking.

We won’t always agree, but please comment constructively and cordially as per the sites guidelines. The goal should not be victory, but rather progress.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text mentions that statins, prescribed to reduce cardiac arrests, can increase the risk of type 2 diabetes and that their benefits have been overstated. This implies a negative stereotype or suspicion

input text:
What am I doing wrong? Hi everyone. I've been on Ozempic for 22 weeks today, along with a fifteen minute walk every other day and monitoring my calories - and my weight has stayed virtually exactly the same. I'm still almost at the highest I've ever been, which is 352lbs (measured daily). I'm on .5mg of the drug, I inject into my abdominal fat once a week. I also have polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS), sleep apnea (now being treated with CPAP) and type 2 diabetes, if that's relevant. Am I doing something wrong? What am I missing?

I'm so sick of nothing working, and of suffering the stigma, pain and disrespect of being this overweight. I've never had a real adult life because I've always considered myself "too overweight" and I'm so sick of it. 

If anyone has any advice or solutions, I'd really appreciate it. 

(If I've broken any rules, feel free to delete this)

EDIT: Thank you all so much for the responses!!! I will be speaking with my doctor about a higher dose tomorrow, and (as some suggested) taking the big step to go to the gym (which is terrifying but I'll deal with it lol) I also will not be weighing myself daily anymore. Again, thanks to everyone who responded!!! 

EDIT 2: wow, I really didn't expect this many people to respond!! From the bottom of my heart, I thank each and every one of you for caring enough to give me your advice. I won’t get fully into the long and bitter story of how I came to be this weight, but part of it is sensory problems with food due to autism, as well as chronic pain that started when i was a healthy weight. I also want to say to anyone reading this who's also struggling with weight issues, change is possible and wonderful, but cannot be fueled by self-hate. I know I can't hate myself into a shape I can love, no matter how much weight I lose. Again, thanks so much to all of you for your support and encouragement!!!!
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author expresses internalized stigma ("I've always considered myself 'too overweight'"), describes experiencing stigma and disrespect due to weight (which is linked to diabetes), and highlights

input text:
Why is there so much pushback against c section by the NHS? I just had my elective c section and it will be happening in 2 weeks. I am quite excited, yet terrified at the same time. I had an obstetrics appointment today where the c section was booked, but of course not before I was told all of the most negative outcomes of c section and almost scared into it. I understand that it is their job to lay out the risks, however, had I chosen induction (which was my other option), i bet none of the risks of induction would have been stated. 

I will be giving birth at 37 weeks as I have gestational diabetes and I am on insulin, so my options were induction or c section. I do not want to have an induction due to the many horror stories I’ve read and the fact that it often results in an emergency c section anyways. I want to have a quick, easy and controlled birth. However, the NHS keeps trying to scare me into thinking how horrible c section is. I had another meeting with a consultant 2 weeks ago who also said she recommends a c section in my case but I guess she didnt add it in her notes because it seems everything is so hush hush when it comes to this topic. So today when I mentioned it to the obstetrician she gave me a weird look and said “no, we do not recommend that - it says in your notes you want it, not that we recommend it”. Another midwife who was showing me the labour ward also said that it probably would be best for me to have a c section and she understands my decision as to why I would prefer that over induction - of course that was basically whispered just in case she isnt heard by anybody. Why is there such stigma? 

I am someone who gets so easily terrified and I am so paranoid about complications so the fact they keep bringing this up has now made me insanely stressed. They said c section is riskier for me because of my higher BMI (37), yet I’ve always heard that higher BMI is associated with a higher likelihood of a c section. Same with GD - not that it’s impossible to give birth vaginally, but there are more complications associated with that like big baby, shoulder dystocia, etc. But none of the pros of a c section for my case have been mentioned. They only keep telling me how further pregnancies could implant into the scar and I could bleed out badly to the point of having a hysterectomy. I am now sitting here with a lot of anxiety thinking “what if I end up dying”. I am aware there are so many positive stories of c sections with no issues whatsoever, but that extra constant pressure has made me so irrational and anxious. 

The c section is now booked already but I also have an appointment for birth options on Monday (there was an issue with scheduling so this got delayed). I dont even know if i want to go if all they would do is once again present all of the horrible c section outcomes  I am stressed enough as it is and I dont want to sit there and listen to further propaganda against it. Did any of you have positive experience of that appointment? I already have the surgery scheduled and i wont change my mind so if the appointment is just about pros/cons (probably only cons as they seem to not favour it) I dont have desire to go.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author perceives negative attitudes and extra scrutiny from healthcare providers due to having gestational diabetes and a higher BMI, anticipating stigma and feeling anxiety as a result

input text:
Obesity Nonsense (2): Fat people deserve better than 'body positivity' Whilst society did its best to stigmatise fat people, a number of left wing voices came out to put the case for body positivity. 

Body positivity wanted fat people to accept their body no matter the size ; get on with life as normal and  for society to stop stigmatising and design life to cater for fat people's needs. Sometimes it went further to claim that stigma itself is more damaging that fatness ; even further to say that fat is not that harmful to health (I think there are too many studies ; anecdotal evidence pointing to the contrary). 

OK that sounds well intended, to make fat people's lives better, what could possibly be wrong with that? 

I am here to argue this trend goes against a fat person's interests:

- the health consequences of obesity are real, and painful and life diminishing ; generally appear later in life. Whilst life expectancy may be similar to normal weight folk, health span ; care needs are certainly not (I speak as a child of one obese parent ; one diabetic parent, both now in their 70s managing multiple conditions. They take over 10 pills a day and spend their lives at doctor's appointments. I am grateful for the subsidised health care they receive, but this is no way to live!). We deserve better than that. 

- diagnosed conditions aside, dealing with the small unpleasantries of living in a fat body is bad enough - running out breath? Chaffing? Lack of energy? Reduced mobility?  Just the constant hunger and need to eat? We deserve better than that. 

- if all stigma went away tomorrow and society helpfully catered to our needs, it would be nice, but the two points above would still not go away. 

Why would you, as a fat person, want to live with these very real physical limitations instead of trying to cure obesity and rid yourself and others of it? Just because you don't want to be blamed for your obesity (like you would not be blamed for say - cancer) ; don't seem to be able to fix it by youself, that does not mean you should accept it. 

Surely understanding and eradicating obesity must be the real, progressive goal here. We have fixed health problems before (infectious diseases, lead contamination, rickets, etc.) and we should do it again, with obesity, diabetes ; heart disease.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text links diabetes and obesity, reinforcing negative stereotypes about both. It suggests that living with obesity or diabetes is undesirable and implies blame for not "fixing"

input text:
Hateful diabetes comments So as a lot of you are aware, there's a lot of stigma that goes around when it relates to diabetes. A lot of people make jokes about having diabetes because you're fat or having diabetes because you're unhealthy. Some of this could be seen as regular humor, while some of it can be viewed in a harassing and offensive manor. Sometimes people will say things like this to a diabetics face. Most of the time when people do this they're intentionally trying to bring the diabetic down and bully them. I'm wondering if people here believe we should throw these comments into the category of hate speech, because we know hate speech is a negative thing and affects people in many bad ways. Some people believe hate speech should be completely illegal, so should these comments themselves be illegal? I'm genuinely curious to hear what people's thoughts are on this topic.

Edit: I'm actually a full supporter of free speech and don't believe jokes or comments that I mentioned above should be anything remotely close to illegal. Sure, sometimes they can be mean or hurtful, but shouldn't be censored. When I was first diagnosed years ago and went onto reddit and discord servers a lot of people seemed to be in favor of censorship when it came to comments or jokes. Now that I've redownloaded reddit I was genuinely curious to see where people stand on this topic, because from what I remember years ago people were very hostile to the jokes.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses common negative stereotypes (e.g., linking diabetes to being "fat" or "unhealthy"), describes direct bullying,

input text:
Body Positivity in the T2D Community Let me start by saying I am not here to judge anyone who does or does not diet, want to lose weight, etc. 

Type 2 diabetes is steeped with stigma, particularly [fat  and  It is widely thought that getting type 2 diabetes is a personal failing, despite the fact that [you can’t “give yourself”  The response to most people with T2D is usually “diet and lose weight” even though most “diets” have been proven to be ineffective for most people in the long term.

While those of us living with type 2 are much more likely to be sympathetic to fellow type 2s, I’ve noticed a lot of talk about fatness, bodies, and dieting that play into the same stigmas and misinformation I shared above. There is dubious at best information on the long term effects of diets like keto or intermittent fasting, and even the idea that being fat is inherently bad for you is  I find the pervasiveness of this talk to be kind of a bummer within a community that is particularly vulnerable to the negative effects of stigma.

I was wondering if anyone else has felt this way, or has any good resources on body positivity and the anti diet movement specifically relating to diabetes. The podcast Maintenance Phase is one of my favorites for learning about all of the misinformation in health and wellness overall but would love something more specific.

EDIT: Cleaned up some of the links
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses widespread negative stereotypes (personal failing, fatness) and misinformation about type 2 diabetes, highlighting awareness of stigma (perceived), concern about

input text:
One Year into the Journey 11.4 to 5.3. A year ago (March 22nd) I received the news that I joined the club no one wanted to be in. I had gone to the doctor for the first time in a decade after going from 330lbs to 240lbs, through walking and doing Calories In Calories out for 3ish years. I avoided the Doctor since i assumed all they could say was "lose weight" so in my mind, them telling me to lose another 20 or so pounds rather than 110 was better for me to achieve that and they would say "keep it up!" and send me on my way. I also was a Dad. My daughter was 10 months at the time, i was 35, finally gave into my wife pressuring me to go. Got my blood work done, everything good, but then bam, 11.4 A1C, 180g fasting glucose, 700+ Triglycerides . Life Changed.

I thought my life was over. Really hard to a father of a 10 month old. Thought I had destined her to experience the sadness that comes with a parent with limitations and death at a young age, rather than  experiencing that as an adult like we all hope as parents. I could barely look at myself in the mirror and found it even harder to look at her. That took a few days. I stumbled upon this subreddit and started reading

***"best thing that ever happened to me"*** ya right, thats BS. Then I started to see it more frequently. Started to research more and more. Became a little too obsessive about reading about it and only talking about it.

Went to my follow up meeting, Doctor put me on 500mg of metformin to go up to 2000mg over 6 weeks as well as a statin taken every night. Fine I can do that. Prescribed a finger prick testing kit and 100 strips. Set up seeing a nutritionist as well.

I have done keto in the past, saw how it impacted a lot of people and jumped on that. Within a week I am starting to see 140s post eating, Start walking the dog 2x per day (2 miles total) instead of our daily .6 miler. Start tracking calories and going low carb. Starting lifting in my basement (bought a adjustable bench and adjustable dumbbells that go up to 200lbs (100 in each arm). Became obsessive over my numbers and testing upwards of 10 times per day....realized that my insurance covers only 1 strip per day, so i go OTC via amazon and contour next. I let the doctor know, we decide to stop at 1000mg of metformin a day. Keep grinding and 3 months later, i'm at 6.1 and down 20 lbs. Reduce statin to 1x every other day.

I continue this and to make it short. 3 months later, I go down to 5.4 down 20 lbs, then finally in january i hit 5.3 down 8 lbs. Doctor reduced my metformin to 500mg . During this time I also went on a cruise, had my birthday, had thanksgiving, had the holidays, and still had an amazing time and kept my numbers how they should be. I now only test fasting and before bed or if I am feeling off. I changed my autoship 75 strips from 2 weeks to once a month.

Point of all this is, it's possible. You will experience anger, depression, and feeling of worthlessness at diagnosis, but just adjust your sails. It's a journey.

Things I have realized and changes.

\- Food is not the point of everything. I was going to become a chef when i was younger, so i have the knowledge of how to adjust foods to make them work. I learned a lot of this from the internet. It's out there.

\- Exercise is amazing. Walking in the subzero temps of michigan is tough, but you feel so good after. Add in lifting and you will be amazed how your body looks in a year. One downside is buying all new clothes.

\- Alcohol. I was a weekend warrior. Always low carb even before diagnosis stuck to mich ultras and straight liquor. Now, i realized how much my life revolved on weekend anticipation and the following days hungover. I still have a few drinks once or twice a month. Not a fifth over the weekend.

\- Social life. A lot of my social life revolved around going out for drinks or partaking in pub food. Now, I have become more active in volunteer groups and physical activity groups. I did lose some friends or maybe I'm not invited out as much due to me not drinking. I am okay with this.

\- Mindfulness. Experiencing a diagnosis like this at a young age brings things into perspective. It made me want to go out and enjoy life, take the adventure, not watch things that only bring stress (politics, i still catch up but MSNBC is not my background sound anymore), and connecting with people through activities

\- Support is important. This disease has a lot of stigma attached to it. I still don't share often but when I do I find out how common it is. Turns out my mom has gastrointestinal diabetes when she was pregnant. 2 out of 3 uncles have it. All, i thought was that my grandma had it. Turns out, it's pretty common in my family. Places like reddit are also a great support system.  
\-Judgement . Prior to this, I did judge GLP1s and diabetics in general. I was wrong. The tools people use are miracles. Everyone has their own battles and we should support anyway to get healthy.

So overall, it is possible. The year has flown by. The amount of self reflection that has occurred is something I wish would have happened earlier, but I am happy it even happened. This diagnosis caused a lifestyle change, I call it a life sentence to eating healthy and remaining active rather than a death sentence. It has caused me to become a better husband to my wife, father to my daughter (and son in september), better dog owner, better family member, better volunteer, and overall better to myself physically, mentally, and emotionally.

**TL;DR : As of now, this is the best physical, mental, and emotional thing that has ever happened to me. I can say those people were not wrong.**

March 22nd to Jan 31 (last appointment) Stats.

A1c - 11.4 to 5.3  
Mean Avg Glucose - 280  to 106  
Fasting Glucose - 180 to 98  
Triglycerides 719 to 103  
Weight - 245lbs tp 198lbs

Routine  
\~2000 cals a day. 40-50 net carbs avg, with 180g-200g of protein a day, rest is fat  
\-2x 1 mile walks with dog a day  
\- Lifting 4-5 days a week. Breakdown In order of days with comma separating days.  Chest/Tri, Back/Bi, Rest, Legs, Upper Body, Arms, rest  
\- Metformin ER 500mg once a day  
\- Statin 5mg once every other day
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author describes feeling anger, depression, and worthlessness at diagnosis, fearing they had doomed their child, and avoiding doctors due to expected judgment. They also

input text:
My thumb is at the belt notch I used to use, 9 notches over. 59m, started Oz last Sept because doc agreed metformin wasn’t doing enough for my diabetes 2 symptoms to wane. Was at 286lbs, A1c at 7.5 and gaining on both with all the crappy diabetes symptoms. Sugars regularly close to 200, body couldn’t regulate temperatures, no energy even after eating tons of food. Just a terrible quality of life. 

So doc said I could try Oz and in two weeks all the diabetes symptoms were gone. Not hungry all the time any more. Side effects minimal. I am now at 237lbs about 8 months later, so about 50lbs gone . A1c at 5.9 now. Daily blood sugar always at 100 whether I eat or not. Have been using the “4 shots per month at 1mg each” pens for the last 6 months. Haven’t moved up to 2mg because I am still steadily losing. Since I am now below 35 bmi I also can have umbilical hernia surgery. 

This medicine is a true miracle. My diabetes is essentially gone. I truly feel like all those years of blaming myself for “lack of will power” or “exercising but not enough” or whatever was all bullshit because it truly is a chemical imbalance that we have that this medicine fixes. No different than a person needing thyroid medication, for instance. The whole stigma that has gone around knocking people down for taking a MEDICINE THEIR BODY CLEARLY NEEDED, whether for diabetes or for weight loss which leads to better health or whatever is just so sad.  

Gonna be a new world in a couple years when these meds are generics and everyone can feel healthy again who has this hormonal imbalance.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author describes years of self-blame for “lack of will power” and references societal stigma against people taking medication for diabetes or weight loss, indicating both internalized and perceived

input text:
It’s not your fault… but, it’s not mine, either. I have been diabetic for over 15 years. GLPs are the first medication that have worked for me. In 15 years.

I have been obese for 20 years. I have two “success” stories in those 20 years of getting to a “healthy” weight. Each time, my body fought back and I regressed. GLPs are the first medication that creates meaningful weight loss without unsustainable “biggest loser” type effort. 

Coming into these forums and attacking the “other” side of the t2d / obesity debate may make you feel some relief in the moment through an emotional release. I can relate. Not getting our meds is extremely frustrating. But, when we do that, we’re doing so at the cost of others’ emotional states - and specifically not the people at fault. It’s worse than a zero sum game because each attack creates 10 charged responses. 

Everyone here is in such a desperate state that they are willing to risk social stigma (which is criminal) and literal sickness in the form of nausea, extreme fatigue, and serious GI symptoms to get healthier after what has likely been years of physical discomfort, prejudice and social misunderstanding. 

Here’s the reality check: no one in this forum is to blame for supply shortages. Full stop. The manufacturer can’t keep up with demand. Period. 

No reasonable person with the conditions listed above should be expected to voluntarily give up a chance at literal life-changing benefits to benefit people they have never met. 

These shortages will end. Let’s try to support each other as we struggle for now. Unloading your frustration on the “others” in this debate is doing far more harm than good. And, that goes for the pharmacists, too. I’m gotten short with mine - and they have with me. But, it’s important to remember that they aren’t to blame. 

Share your frustration with the people who can actually do something about it… in our case, Eli Lilly. 

Love you all. Good luck on your journeys. And, hang in there!
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text references social stigma as "criminal," mentions years of prejudice and misunderstanding, and highlights the intersection of diabetes and obesity stigma. It also describes the willingness to risk

input text:
Vent: Tired of the stigma but never surprised. I’m just so tired of hearing the age old “tHiS iS fOr diAbEtics” or “yOu’ll gAin iT aLL bAcK” garbage victim mentality. I’m tired of people judging a few D list celebrities (looking at you Bravo housewives) for using it without actually meeting any criteria when the rest of us have struggled to get insurance to pay for it. 

I listen to a lot of different podcasts and the amount of these people without any first hand experience or knowledge sh*tting on this treatment is tiring. 

I’m assuming if you’re a type 2 diabetic taking Wegovy and you’ve been on it before the “craze” you really shouldn’t have trouble finding 2.4mg at this point so they’re just making crap up. 

This med is life changing. We a know that and I’ll keep going but I hate that it’s like the new “ADHD medication stigma” where you can’t tell people you take it because you know they’ll judge you and assume sh*t. 

I’ve lost 60 lbs since end of November. That’s not “rapid” weightloss. I’ve never felt better in my life. I’ll be going off soon to get pregnant but I hate that I can’t just not care and be honest about how I’ve made progress. 

That’s all.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author describes ongoing judgment and negative stereotypes about diabetes and its treatments, anticipates being judged if they disclose medication use, expresses frustration at not

Concern about Girlfriends health I feel so mean posting this I really do. But this comes from a place of genuine concern. I really want to keep all this info super super vague for the most part but I will keep in everything important just for the purpose of anonymity, I don’t want her finding this. 

She’s between 25-30F. She drinks ALOT of soda. I never EVER limit her soda intake, I’ve never told her no you can’t have a soda it’s unhealthy but instead, I offer her a water at the table instead of soda, and occasionally she will be okay with but she tells me she wants a soda instead. A sprite or a coke. 

She has a big tumbler full of soda at work, then fills it up comes home, and drinks it all. Then she will either buy a soda that day, or if we have some, take a soda from the garage. 

My concern comes from the fact that her family is mostly obese or has been extremely overweight at some point. Same thing with my family. And both of our families have a history of diabetes or being pre diabetic. The one thing my family has in common for being diabetic is because of SODA. My grandma drinks 8 cokes a day. Not exaggerating. Same with all of her 5+ sisters. My girlfriends family is the same way and NOW I see her falling into the same pit the our families have fallen into

She loves soda and I love seeing her happy with a drink like that. But she doesn’t go to the gym, she lives a pretty sedentary lifestyle outside of work. She doesn’t eat a ton of food, def not enough for weight gain. I ask her to come with me and she will say no. Occasionally go on walks, but the walks to soda intake ratio is so far off that walks can’t help. Since I’ve noticed this habit, she has gained 40+ lbs in the last year. I don’t have a problem with weight gain I want to make that clear, but she’s reaching obesity with no intention to stop. 

Please help me maybe im overreacting but I don’t know how to bring this up to her, she’s also very sensitive because of the way her ex treated her so I really need a solid way to bring this up to her. One that’s nice, caring and supportive. 

Again! I don’t care about weight gain, I don’t care about the stigma of soda being unhealthy, but I’ve watched all my family drink themselves to pure obesity and type 2 diabetes so I feel that my feelings of concerns are valid. If I am stupid for this please let me know.
label: Yes stigma

input text:
I shouldn't feel guilty for formula feeding...but I do >**EDIT**: Oh my - I didn't know this post would get SO much attention :,) Thank you so much to everyone who has responded with such kind words, advice, and love <3 I am eternally thankful to have found such a supportive community.  
>  
>You have all made me feel so much better <3 thank you a million 

;#x200B;

;#x200B;

It's awful how much stigma there is around formula feeding. I had to switch to exclusive ff because my baby wouldn't latch for more than five minutes and because my milk production wasn't enough.

At first... the nurses would say "It's fine. Never believe that your breasts are empty. Just put the baby on as often as he needs."

So I did.

I would put him on the breast whenever he asked for it which was at that point (2 days pp) every 15-30 minutes because he could never get enough food out.

He would cry himself red from hunger. I would give him both breasts multiple times in one feeding which could last one to two hours at a time.

I wasn't sleeping at all. but that wasn't my main concern. My concern was that my newborn baby was starving.

I went to a lactation consultant multiple times and to the nurses at the postnatal clinic in my province. They thought me how to hold my baby in different positions for latching. They explained that the more he ate the more it created demand for my body and that he was getting enough food even if I thought he didn't.

If they said so, who was I to disagree as a FTM? I knew nothing except my gut feeling told me it wasn't enough.

My baby wouldn't sleep because he was constantly wanting to feed.

I did that dance for one week. Until at my doctor's visit they told me he had lost 13% of his birth weight.

No fucking shit.

He wasn't eating enough and I knew it.

So I started supplementing. Offering breast, then a bottle of formula. It helped for a few days but my baby got used to the bottle and he really did not want to latch on a breast that gave him so little food for so much effort and work anymore.

So I decided to pump exclusively.

I would pump 10 minutes, both sides then take a 20 minute break and do it again. I would do that for five hours in a row. Then I would pump every 2 hours from then on.

I was severely sleep-deprived but I didn't care about that. I was determined to do what everyone was telling me was best.Because "Breast is best" - you have to feed your baby breast milk or they will fail to thrive they said. The pressure was on and when I ended my first five hours of power pumping with only 5ml of breast milk... I broke down.

I was failing.

So after another week of pumping with no improvement...my husband and I made the decision to switch to exclusive formula feeding.

People are telling me "haven't you seen the studies? your baby will get diabetes, he will get sick because he's not getting the antibodies from your breast milk, he will grow up with all kinds of health problems. Formula is so bad for him."

T.T

Although my baby is now peacefully sleeping - happily eating and spending his days contented and cuddling with us because he is finally fed and gaining the appropriate amount of weight since birth (3weeks pp)... I am worried that I am still failing.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text references others warning that formula feeding will cause the baby to get diabetes, reflecting perceived stigma. The author expresses worry and guilt, showing anticipated and internal

Day 2… and hi!  On Day 2 of my starting regimen (2.5) and just wanted to say hi and also thank everyone for this super helpful community. It has really helped me to understand the opportunities this medicine can offer and has reduced some of my personal stigma and doubt about going on a GLP-1.

My hope is to drop some excess weight (39 BMI), maintain that, and get my newly diagnosed pre-diabetes status trending downward. I am lucky to have a very supportive doctor who recommended ZB with dose increases every 4 weeks, monthly tele-health check-ins, and tri-monthly in person check-ins with labs. Insurance is covering things and if all goes well, it looks like I’m in this for the long game. 

Anyway so far, only symptoms since my first dose Monday night seem to be a dry mouth (drinking water like crazy) and maybe some extra tiredness? Hard to tell on the latter, as I’m also a working mom in perimenopause! 

Throughout this journey, I plan to continue my already-very-regular cardio workouts and weight training, so any tips on keeping hydration and energy up would be appreciated!!

input text:
Reached my goal weight!! My positive keto story I don’t usually post in this thread but I just wanted to share my positive experience with keto after hitting my goal weight of 135! Officially down 38 pounds since Jan 2023   are a few before and after photos for anyone interested.

My partner (34m) and I (27f) started keto in January of 2023. Before we started - our energy levels were poor, we accepted that inflammation in our bodies are normal, and that we were just naturally overweight people now. Oh - and that we were doomed to our families health history of diabetes and heart disease. 

Our food lifestyle at the time wasn’t the worst, but our snacking, sugar and use of processed frozen foods creeped up on us over time.

One day my partner learned about Keto from some work buddies and wanted to give it a shot. So we “keto-fied” our kitchen with tons of fresh meat/veg, donated everything else, and never looked back. It was a full 180 lifestyle change.

The benefits of keto are doing wonders for our bodies, mental health, and energy levels - beyond anything I ever thought. We both have learned a lot through this process and it really reshaped our thoughts around nutrition and exercise. I have also never felt more connected to food in a positive way. It has made me appreciate the healing power of food :)

I wish keto didn’t have this stigma of a restricted or unhealthy diet. We try to talk to family and friends, which then we are usually met with eye rolls or “glad you found something that worked for you” - so we try to keep it light.

Anyway - thank you to this community for sharing your stories and providing access to educational resources. They definitely impacted my journey. Stay the course :)
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text mentions feeling "doomed to our families health history of diabetes," implying a negative, fatalistic view of diabetes. It also references the "stigma" around

input text:
Why is Diabetes Still a Taboo? It’s Been 10 Years Since My Diagnosis, But I’m Still Struggling Mentally** Hey everyone, I’m 23 (M)  and I’ve been living with diabetes since I was 13. At that age, I had no idea what it would mean for my life. Honestly, I didn’t even understand what diabetes was back then, but everything changed overnight. I was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes, and that’s when my world flipped upside down.

Over the years, I’ve learned a lot about my condition. My endocrinologist explained that my diabetes likely comes from a genetic mutation, something I might have been born with. It’s not just because I ate too much sugar, which is what a lot of my family seems to think. But somehow, even after 10 years, there’s this constant stigma I feel. Family and relatives are always quick to assume it’s my fault for having too many sweets, and that really messes with my head. Every time they say it, I feel this weight in my chest because I know the truth, but I can’t convince them.

Recently, I went through a tough breakup, and honestly, I think my diabetes played a huge role. It’s hard enough to manage the physical side of things, but emotionally, it’s been draining. My partner didn’t understand my condition, and it created so much tension between us. We tried, but I think the stress of managing diabetes day in and day out without real support took its toll.

And here’s the part that I’m still struggling to understand Why is it still such a taboo topic? When I hear people talk about diabetes, especially in passing, I get so anxious. It’s not just the physical effects that bother me—it’s the mental toll it takes. The stigma and judgment are real, and even now, at 23, I find myself hesitating to open up about it to friends or even potential partners. There’s always this fear of being misunderstood or judged.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text describes direct blame from family (experienced), awareness of negative stereotypes (perceived), fear of judgment and hesitation to disclose (anticipated), and
