input text:
CW // body dysmorphia, mental health, fatphobia I (25y/o, 35 weeks + 1) need this to reach other people who were already plus size/fat before getting pregnant. Also totally open to experiences of people who gained significant weight during pregnancy and have been affected by body dysmorphia. 

I was so woefully unprepared for the level of body dysmorphia that I would experience being plus sized and gaining weight in my stomach while pregnant. I have been larger my whole life, and as an adult had finally gained comfort and peace with my body and the way that it looked. Maybe I was naïve, but the experience of increased fatphobia, and the loss of social privileges that I have experienced due to the changing of my body has been so difficult. At times I feel like my pregnancy is imperceptible due to the weight I carry elsewhere, and it has been devastating for my mental health. 

I feel like it’s so hard to talk about this because of the complications of obesity on pregnancy, the prevalence of gestational diabetes parentheses (which I also have) and the general stigma surrounding fat bodies in our society. I have otherwise had a very healthy pregnancy, and my body (aside from being larger) is otherwise very healthy and carries me through the world and I’m thankful for it. It’s the way that people treat me in public and look at me that has changed and impacted me so negatively. The way that I see myself has changed, my experience of love and intimacy in my relationship has been drastically changed by my insecurities because of my stomach, and I feel like there are no safe spaces to talk about this because of the aforementioned stigma and focus on weight and health consciousness during pregnancy. 

Aside from it being noted in my charts that I have a high BMI, no healthcare provider that I have worked with throughout the duration of my pregnancy has ever mentioned to me my weight gain nor my starting weight as a concern or a factor in their treatment of me. But the social implications and the experiences of moving through the world in my clothing and in my body that was already large and having my stomach look the way that it does currently is honestly the worst part of pregnancy for me. 

Not sure if I’m looking for advice or simply understanding, I just needed to get this shit off my chest. I’m so ready to give birth and start exercising regularly again and eating healthier, but I wish my mind wasn’t so preoccupied with future weight loss and I could just enjoy this experience, but I can’t. I’m uncomfortable physically and I’m so exhausted and I’m even more tired because of my perception of myself and my weight being the heaviest I have ever been.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses the stigma and negative societal attitudes toward fat bodies, especially during pregnancy, and references gestational diabetes as part of the broader stigma

input text:
Guilt and Shame in the T1D Community One thing I've noticed about the T1D Community is the amount of internalized shame/guilt rampant in our members. I think it's a reflection of a greater stigma our society has towards diabetics and it's something I think we need to reckon with as a community. Ask yourself these questions:

1. Have you ever felt embarrassed to share blood sugar data with your endocrinologist? 
2. How do people in your life address your "bad" diabetes days? Are they quick to attach negative adjectives to how you handle your care? IE when you have a bad day is the focus automatically on HOW you messed up? 
3. Do you ever feel guilty for slipping on essential care?
4. Do any of these feelings/conversations manifest in an improved quality of self-care? 

The main point I'm hoping to drive home is that no other disease (outside of active addictions) attaches as much blame on the individual as is done to diabetics. And therefore, this type of shame/guilt is unique to diabetes; it comes with the diagnosis. 

With that in mind, it is important to note that not everybody responds to same stimulus in the same way. The same shameful feeling that motivates one diabetic to take their care seriously might cause another diabetic to spiral. Therefore I think it's important we as a community do our best to use neutral/positive language in relation to describing behavior / giving action items.

Ex. instead of "Your numbers are atrocious, you must feel terrible. How do you live like this?" Say "I see that you're struggling with your blood sugar and I know that doesn't feel good. What can we do to get you back on track?"

I would love to have a discussion about this. Let me know if you agree or disagree
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses widespread shame and guilt within the T1D community, reflecting internalized stigma. It also highlights perceived societal stigma and the anticipation of blame or

input text: Representation is always boring  As a type one diabetic, I have had it with my only representation being random characters in the background or people being mistaken for drug users. I hate it. I want stupid, funny representation! Like one of those cheesy, teenie vampire romances but the human that the vampire is in love with is just diabetic. Like, when their blood sugar is way too high, to their vampire love interest it's like drinking a Capri-sun. Just imagine how much stupidly funny shit you could write if you weren't afraid to use disability in a real but funny way of representation. Not only in type one of course but generally, though I can't speak for other disabilities. I just want some relatable but funny content, to lift the stigma that disabled folks always have to be miserable or background characters or can't be funny. Give us some comedic representation to help show that I can have fun too, treating my insulin pump like an annoying af Tamagotchi or being a cyborg that can't get through airport security! Just some fun antics that make us human. Because we are.

input text:
Bias I was really shocked today when I read a post about someone being forced off Ozempic and several people replied along the lines of “diabetes is more important to treat than weight”.

Having spent since I was knee-high on a dialysis ward, and having diabetes in my fam, I am intimately acquainted with the seriousness of diabetes.  But to say that it is more serious than being overweight is patently untrue.  The leading causes of death are directly linked to excess weight.

Dr. Sue Pedersen an Endocrinologist had the following to say “Some countries have restricted availability to people with type 2 diabetes.  As the authors note, this implies that obesity is somehow less important to treat than diabetes, trivializing the benefits of weight management medication, and propagating weight stigma and bias.

 Others have objected to weight management medication outright, stating that we should be preventing obesity rather than treating it.  Authors draw a powerful analogy : Would we ever consider preventing cancer, but not treat it?   Of course not.  We need to do both. Full stop.”
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses the perception that diabetes is prioritized over obesity, implying that obesity is less important to treat. It highlights how this attitude trivializes obesity and propagates weight stigma,

input text:
The Curious Case of Type 2 Recovery - Or Why My Glucose Meter Is Bracing For Impact Hi! 

Type 1 here, with a family forest of both types. I know I'll get the classic "eat a normal diet and it'll come back" responses, so let's dig into this metabolic mystery.

What exactly is a "normal diet" anyway? The Standard American Diet is probably too much for ANY human's metabolism, diabetes or not. We're basically saying "if you return to eating an excessive diet that's unhealthy for everyone, your diabetes might return."

Well... yeah. That's like saying:

- "If you recovered from liver failure, maybe don't drink excessively"

- "If your broken leg healed, maybe don't jump off buildings"

- "If you recovered from alcoholism, maybe don't start binge drinking"

- "If you beat cancer, maybe don't start chain smoking"

Yet we don't say these people are "in permanent remission" - we say they recovered but need to maintain healthy habits. Like everyone else.

Here's my challenge about true recovery vs "permanent remission":

If someone has:

- Maintained normal blood sugars for years
- Handles moderate carbs like any other healthy person
- Only needs to avoid excessive habits (like everyone should)
- Shows normal insulin sensitivity
- Has no metabolic abnormalities

Why do we insist they're still "*in remission*" rather than recovered? If someone recovered from liver failure 60 years ago and maintained perfect health since, we don't say they're at higher risk than anyone else their age who develops liver problems. Their liver regenerated. They healed.

The broken leg analogy really hits home here - we don't say someone was "never cured" of a broken leg just because they need to be careful not to traumatize it again. That's just common sense for everyone's legs, previously broken or not!

I've been downvoted before for this view, but as someone who literally can't produce insulin, I understand diabetes severity intimately. I'm not suggesting miracle cures or minimizing T2's seriousness. I'm questioning whether we're stuck in outdated thinking that might be causing unnecessary psychological burdens.

Let me be clear:- I fully understand that some people will struggle with T2 despite their best efforts, and many deal with complex comorbidities that make management more challenging. But I wonder if our insistence on calling it "lifelong and progressive" is partially attached to the unfair stigma of T2 being solely a "lifestyle disease." 

It's fascinating how strongly some people defend the "always in remission" stance - perhaps overcompensating for this stigma? The science shows that for many people, it's neither necessarily lifelong nor inevitably progressive. And here's another thought - our ancestors naturally went through periods of feast and famine, high and low carb availability. Metabolic flexibility - the ability to adapt to different fuel sources - is actually a sign of good health. Having periods of lower carb intake or intermittent fasting isn't a "restricted diet," it's probably closer to how our metabolism evolved to function optimally.

This isn't about dividing people into "tried hard enough" v.  "didn't try hard enough" camps. It's about accurately understanding recovery and healing  and not letting stigma influence our medical terminology.

 Some people might always need medication or stricter management, and that's completely valid.

 But for others who've achieved true metabolic restoration, maybe it's time to acknowledge that as actual recovery.

Maybe we're confusing:

- Initial susceptibility to T2
- With some kind of permanent "metabolic scarring" that might not actually exist after years of healthy function

Ready for the spiraling numbers - at least on my meter, I know how to handle those! 

(Disclaimer: This post pairs well with monitoring your blood sugar, not with Facebook posts about cinnamon cures...)

Thoughts? Experiences? Counter-arguments? Let's have a real discussion about this.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The author discusses the stigma around type 2 diabetes, particularly the belief that it is solely a "lifestyle disease" and the psychological burden of labeling it as "lifelong

input text:
Disability in Medicine: rampant, but overlooked! What do the following have in common:an attending cardiac anesthesiologist with Tourette’s syndrome, a medical student with a brain tumor, a Derm PGY3 with OCD, a surgical chief resident who abused alcohol as an intern, but then found sobriety, a rheumatologist with relapsing MS?
They are all disabled and are afforded the protections outlined by the Americans with Disabilities Act.
This is just the tip of the iceberg. Throw in the estimated  forty percent of burned out clinicians, diseases of aging and the most prevalent medical conditions among US adults(diabetes, arthritis, CAD), and th numbers become staggering!
I have been founding a nonprofit called Doctors with Disabilities, to create a support network for docs facing illness or disability. This came about, after I had a stroke and found myself feeling alone, avoided by colleagues and marginalized.In the course of doing so, I have encountered the individuals described..
Let’s face it. We treat each other porky when it comes to illness. Woe to the surgeon who is too ill to show up for his/her block time or the IM attending who missed a clinic because of the onset of an unexpected GI illness. During my surgical Residency, absence of any sort was automatically assumed to be goofing off. Residents would snicker behind the back of a med student who needed to go home to lie down because of a migraine. I had the flu, once during my residency, on April 5th. I returned to work, only to face a rousing round of accusations that I had stayed home to work on getting m ty tax return s submitted (untrue... embarrassing ly, I did not submit any tax return s for several years during Residency-just too overwhelmed!)
So, why are we so unkind to ailing peers? I suggest that it has become an ingrained part of the Medical community of practice.
Everyone is so strung out that he/she does not have the energy to graciously cover one more single night of call for Ann I'll team member. We feel compelled to indoctrinate younger housestaff and medical students with the same warped lens.
It needs to change..our whole attitude that is, so we can embrace the tenets of the ADA-offering reasonable accommodation d to those who truly need it!
It does patients a disservice when Disabilities become a stigma among Physiciand themselves
Not meant to be a rant, but a provocative commentary to elicit your feedback! Thanks.
Le VB
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text discusses how prevalent medical conditions like diabetes are included in a list of disabilities that lead to marginalization and avoidance by colleagues. It highlights both the perception and experience

input text:
recently diagnosed sibling Not sure where else to go with this but here. My younger sister was just diagnosed with T1D at a checkup she went to earlier today due to some of the telltale concerning symptoms, but my whole family was hoping something else was causing them and despite the possibility at the back of our minds, we were taken aback by how suddenly she was rushed to the ER and how quickly her life has already changed as a result of the diagnosis.

I’m not affected other than being her older sibling and wanting to support her. She wasn’t in DKA when she was taken to the ER, but she had sugar levels ~400. No ketones detected to my knowledge. It’s good that it was caught early, but I think my whole family is processing the fact that under 12 hours ago she was just at school like any other day, and now her life is going to change so dramatically due to the constant monitoring etc.

I guess I just want to hear from you all. She’s in high school and of course has so much ahead of her, but I want to know how to best support her. To those here, when you were newly diagnosed, what helped you the most? I don’t live at home since I’m older so there’s a limited capacity of things I can do, but what changes should I make as a sibling to help her accept the ‘new normal’? And what advice would you give to a newly, spontaneously diagnosed type 1 diabetic teenager, so I can pass it along and give her some encouragement?

I’m already heartbroken because she’s mentioned concerns that people will bully her at school due to the stigma surrounding diabetes and I hate that’s even a possibility. :(

Throughout our lives we’ve always joked about how she’s a ‘little clone’ of me since we’re so similar. I’ve always been able to give her suggestions when she had life questions, but this is something I have no experience with, so any advice you may have regarding how to help things become more routine would be great.

It feels weird to be posting here when I’m not even the one diagnosed, but I want to help her and have absolutely no idea how. This is new for all of us and I’d really appreciate any wisdom from you lovely people.

Thanks <3
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text expresses concern that the sister may be bullied at school due to the stigma surrounding diabetes, indicating both an anticipation of stigma and an awareness that negative stereotypes exist. The

input text:
While non-autistic people cash on our autism (autism mom merch, charity merch), I can't imagine an autistic person cash grabbing by doing "autism labour". I spent my two brain cells on this title! These things are hard to put to words, but I hope I can English out my point of view.

There was a post about "[autism high  recently that made me feel things, lol. It's mentioning a mother of autistic child selling print on demand merch with "autism acceptance" logo on different goods. I don't blame her for making a living any way she can while caring for a disabled kid - do whatever you need to survive in this economy, dude! But I still have feelings about double standards in this type of commerce and I'll allow myself to discuss them.

This allistic mom can sell "autism heels" because her child is autistic and she's an ally, that money would buy him food. This product's main value is this "autism support" both in terms of monetary support of autistic individuals (indirectly) and overcoming stigma. Autism merch looks appropriate to general public. But if her son grows up to become a cobbler and make some nice shoes, how would it look if he put out his professional work as "autism shoes"? I honestly can't imagine that.

People with disabilities don't make extra profit by adding their disability label to the labour they provide for money. No "diabetic accountant", "cerebral palsy plumber" or "epileptic dentist" is going to get more money simply for the label. I once did autism construction engineering, lol, doesn't it inspire confidence?

I understand there's a difference between autism-themed and autistic-produced product in my previous examples, but I have more.

My birth country has a very beloved allistic managed autism organisation that sells cute/inspirational postcards and items with silly quotes of autistic adults in wonky font. I can't imagine selling my "autistic quotes" as inspirational and cute, deliberately framing how "pure" and "special" I am. Autism seems to be an appropriate marketable product only when removed from an autistic carrier and handled as an observed curiosity by a "normal" person.

I am a professional, autistic, I suffer from stigma and I'd like to be supported and included and acknowledged as an autistic person who creates value. But I can't imagine non-ironically selling "autism earrings" or offering "autism metalwork classes". It would sound comedic and reductive. If anything, it's autistic people who should have been granted support, inclusion and monetary benefits by attaching autism sticker to their work. But instead it is perceived  to discredit our professionalism, humanity and agency because of infantalization. While people who use the label on our behalf don't get to compromise their professional image, their clients won't question if they are capable to provide service.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text references "no 'diabetic accountant'," highlighting that people with diabetes (and other disabilities) are not afforded extra value or

input text:
Dave’s hot chicken syringe If you’ve spent any time around fast food culture or Gen Z social media lately, you’ve likely seen brands going out of their way to be bold, edgy, and “in on the joke.” But sometimes, the line between edgy and outright dangerous isn’t just crossed—it’s obliterated.

Enter Dave’s Hot Chicken’s latest promotion: “Davezembic.” Yes, syringes—real ones—are being handed out as part of a campaign that riffs on Ozempic, the diabetes drug turned weight loss craze. What was once a medical treatment for a serious condition has become the centerpiece of a fast-casual brand’s viral marketing stunt.

Let’s be crystal clear: this is not clever. It’s not just tone-deaf—it’s irresponsible.

Ozempic (and similar GLP-1 medications) has become a controversial flashpoint in both medical and cultural conversations. The drug is lifesaving for many diabetics. For others, it’s a last-ditch effort in managing obesity. And for some younger users, it’s become a dangerous shortcut, normalized by social media, influencers, and now, apparently, chicken joints.

The message this promotion sends to Dave’s overwhelmingly young demographic is deeply unsettling. It trivializes real medical treatments and turns syringe use—a visual and visceral symbol of medical necessity—into a punchline. For anyone struggling with eating disorders, body image issues, or health-related anxiety, it’s not just a bad joke. It’s a trigger.

Worse, it plays with the visual language of drug use in a country still grappling with opioid addiction, where syringes don’t just mean medicine—they mean danger, stigma, trauma.

And let’s not forget: Dave’s serves food. Really indulgent, crave-worthy food. There’s something disturbingly cynical about a brand marketing high-calorie meals while nodding to a medication that suppresses appetite. It’s like selling beer with a side of hangover remedy—or worse, glamorizing the problem while mocking the solution.

Marketing has power. And with power comes responsibility. Dave’s Hot Chicken is beloved by many and has a unique ability to shape trends and influence youth culture. That power shouldn’t be used to reduce serious health concerns into TikTok fodder.

This isn’t about being humorless or overly sensitive. It’s about recognizing that some topics—like medical treatment, mental health, and drug imagery—require care, not gimmicks. A syringe, in the hands of a brand, should never be a prop.

Dave’s, do better. Because this time, the heat isn’t just in the chicken. It’s in the consequences of your choices.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text highlights how a marketing campaign trivializes diabetes treatment (Ozempic), turning a serious medical necessity into a joke. It notes the stigmatizing impact on

Welp- they knocked me back to daily’s. I had lost my SSRI bottle, (I keep them in a daily take pill container) I had figured last months bottle would be ok, as the rule book didn’t really specify it wouldn’t, as it’s exactly the same amount, Mg, etc. my count was accurate. So realistically- I won’t be back on my take homes till next Monday. All cuz the bottle isn’t this months- but the pills were. It’s an anti-depressant. The Xanax was accurate + extra..

I’m switching clinics, one that’ll have a counselor. I now gotta delay starting my new job at my local MAT clinic cuz of this. Idc if this comes off entitled. I’m just frustrated- I’m doing everything else I should be. Getting, well trying, to start this new job, looking into college, the counts were correct- even had extra for the Xanax. But because the SSRI was IN last months bottle, I gotta come in daily until my next apt, on Thursday. I’m just frustrated. 

They wouldn’t do this for a diabetic or any other type of health issue? Why is punishment in healthcare acceptable just for addiction? Gotta love how that stigma still permeates even in the industry workers supposed to make you better - sorry, just frustrated ; venting. Now gotta find a job new clinic that’s close to me, that has Counselors, I guess.

I am a mom of two. My oldest son has type one diabetes. He was diagnosed almost exactly two years ago now. He's on the pump with the C G M combo. Before my son's diagnosis, I had a busy career in marketing and tech. However, after his diagnosis during the lockdowns, I took a medical leave. After a two-month break, I realized I wasn't ready to go back to work. A year later, I joined Diabetes Canada as a contract role for an advocacy campaign. This was a good opportunity for me to step back into my career while being passionate about it. I'm based in Waterloo, Ontario.

I've been curious about how your child's diagnosis affected them and you as well. The journey has been challenging from a stigma point of view, but also experientially. Your child's diagnosis had a big impact on you, and I'd like to hear more about that.

input text:
PAD-Parent of T1 Child: I never saw myself as a stay-at-home mom, so it really impacted my identity at the time. I struggled with that. I had a very busy career, traveling a lot for work. But if it hadn't happened during the pandemic, when my kids were at home online learning and my husband and I were trying to work from home, I don't know if I would've stopped working completely. It just became too much. I thought, right now I can only focus on this. It was a difficult decision, but one that I felt like had to happen. I had stigmas around stay-at-home moms and it forced me to think about gender roles a lot. Which became very defined in our family all of a sudden. I saw myself as a nurse, cleaning lady, property manager, or sometimes a therapist. The mental health component was not discussed when he was diagnosed at the hospital. There was shame that I felt - what did I do? Was it because he had antibiotics as a baby? Or did he catch COVID and trigger this autoimmune disorder?

I've had some counseling. We put my son in counseling right away, as he already had anxieties around food due to allergies. The counseling has helped with those issues. Now that he's getting older, we're dealing with different things. I think the hospital gave us a social worker, but I only had one 15-minute conversation with her because there are many kids who need to talk to her and can't afford therapy or access benefits plans.

Interviewer 1: No, really helpful and I appreciate your willingness to disclose what can be very personal experiences. One of my curiosities is whether you were feeling external pressures that added to those internalized shoulds on yourself? Were there external things that triggered that or not?
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The parent expresses shame and self-blame about their child's diabetes, questioning if their actions caused it (internalized). They mention stigmas around stay-at-home

input text:

Interviewer 1 : With what you now know is really false information, how could we change the conversation to be supportive? Somebody like yourself entering an experience without the same kind of background doesn't make sense. How do we change that conversation so it's supportive?

input text:
PAD-Parent of T1 Child: I think more time with a professional social worker during those three days in the hospital would have been helpful. They could have validated our feelings and provided a better explanation of the science behind it. A logical explanation would have been sufficient. The week before my son was diagnosed, we met a woman at a family party who had developed type one diabetes in her thirties. She suspected that stress and trauma from being a refugee to Canada triggered her condition. I thought maybe our son's trigger was something we did or the stress we put him under, but logically I knew it wasn't his diet. It's hard not to blame yourself when you're unsure about what caused it. Every conversation with our healthcare team focused on managing blood sugar levels rather than how we were feeling.

Interviewer 1: Really valuable information and insight. Thank you. I'm curious about the external pressures of being a stay-at-home mom and how that might have played into your experience. Did you experience stigma related to that? Was it what others said, or was it about what you were doing to yourself?
label: Yes stigma

input text:
PAD-Parent of T1 Child: I think so. I think people don't really recognize the amount of work it takes to manage diabetes, insulin dependent, especially diabetes. People were up several times a night, like they had brain fog, like they had a newborn again. Except at least with a newborn, you know, this will end. But when you have a child with type one diabetes, all you can think about is, this is going to be my life forever. And when I'm no longer taking care of him, I'll be worried about him. Nobody can really fully understand that unless they're living through it. So I was on a lot of Facebook like parent support groups, and that was very helpful just in validating the way I felt because this was not just me. This is there are other moms, mostly moms, and that's the other part I really struggled with is those gender roles.

And to be honest, I do 97% of the diabetes management in our household. And a lot of that is because I made the decision to quit my job to do it. My husband took a very hands-off approach. That led to all kinds of resentments, right? As you can imagine, if you're in a relationship where you kind of have to parent equally together, and then you throw in the type one diagnosis and one parent takes control of it, the dynamics change. That was a piece that I could have never seen coming, right? And I'm sure there's lots of resentments there that I carry, even though I made the decision to do this. But you can't help but feel it. In those moments of sleepless nights and maybe you're not eating right, you're not exercising, you're not getting enough time for yourself, it's only natural to kind of feel angry about all of that. And I don't think everybody in our circle was supportive. They understood why I was going to stay home. There was like a really important reason there. Even my manager was very understanding. But he was very shocked when I told him after the two months that I wasn't coming back. He had made plans for me to be back.

Interviewer 2: Something you need to do?
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The parent perceives a lack of understanding and support from others regarding the burden of diabetes management, feels isolated, and struggles with gendered expectations. There is also

input text:
PAD-Parent of T1 Child: It would be helpful if people understood that it's an autoimmune disorder. The condition is so variable, and no two days are the same. I can leave a plan for caregivers to follow, including what he should eat and how much insulin he needs. However, unless you're living with this, you can't truly understand its impact on his mood. When he's high, he becomes very moody, which can be misinterpreted as bad behavior by outsiders. As he goes through puberty, it's especially challenging for him. A better understanding of the disease would help alleviate some of the stigma associated with it.

Interviewer 1: What can you tell us about the stigma your son might have experienced in school? I'm sure, as a mother who's close to helping your son, you must know about these experiences.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The parent discusses how her son's mood changes due to diabetes are often misinterpreted as bad behavior by others, indicating awareness of negative judgments (perceived stigma) and concern

input text:
PAD-Parent of T1 Child: Yeah. And I asked him this morning too, I told him I was doing, we're doing a special project because he knows I work for Diabetes Canada. So, he said that he doesn't feel like he experiences a stigma except when people, especially kids, his peers don't understand that there's a type one and a type two diabetes. And he says he doesn't mind asking questions. He felt that the questions are genuine, just kids being curious. But that he really hates it when people assume he's eating too much sugar. And that, so I, when he was talking, I could hear his own stigmas coming out. He says, I am healthy and I eat well. I am not like a type two diabetic who didn't take care of themselves.
And so it's like, oh geez, how do we stop this when somebody living with diabetes identifies themselves this way? So he said that the biggest impact is people not understanding that this is an autoimmune disorder. And he even said, I wish they didn't call it diabetes. They should have a different name for this. So that's really all he's talked about. I know for him though, his friends will be over and I'll hear his pump is beeping and he needs to do something about his blood sugar, but he doesn't want to because he's with his friends and he has said, that he already feels so different than his peers. Like even before, without the diabetes, he already feels so different than his peers.
And then the diabetes just makes him feel even more different. He can't just jump in the pool. He's got to check his pump, turn off his pump, take off his pump, make sure it's not going to get water on it. He's got band-aids, he's got markings on his body that when he goes swimming, other people can see. When he is normal day life, it's invisible a lot of the time and he wants to keep it invisible. I know he wants to keep it invisible. I did force him to go to Pump Couture last weekend. I think that was a good experience for him.
Interviewer 1 : It looked like it would be a fabulous event. I haven't, I mean, I obviously didn't go. I'm out in Vancouver, but, it's quite a lot of fun.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The child perceives stigma from peers' misunderstandings and stereotypes (e.g., assumptions about sugar intake), internalizes negative beliefs about type

input text:
PAD-Parent of T1 Child: Well, I suppose because this was kind of top of mind, I knew this meeting was coming. So over the weekend we were with some family and I noticed that the other parent there was very gracious in saying what kind of drinks were available for the kids. Often it's like my youngest one is given juice or soda, but they know Theo has diabetes and it's okay if he has the sugar-free juice. And I know they mean well, but it just makes him feel different than his brother or other kids. The thing is a lot of people don't realize that if you're on insulin, you can eat what you want; you have the right to choose what you want to eat, and that's what insulin is for.

The other side of it is we try to follow a really low-carb diet. When he was on MDI, because it was really difficult, especially going to bed to manage, if we were going to have hamburgers or pizza, oh god, pizza at dinner time meant I was up all night trying to fix it. So for my own benefit, I was like, we are moving to a low-carb dinner. And then I was on those parent groups and the amount of nasty things that people can say, how dare you give your child a low-carb lifestyle or diet, don't know your child needs carbs? People just want to make everything their business and they know nothing about the science of food or the science of insulin or the reasons why parents might be trying this for their child's health, for their mental health.

And then even yesterday, we let my son have diet soda because he wants soda. He realizes that when he has soda, life is hard because his blood sugar goes really high and he doesn't like that feeling. But he also wants to be able to have what his brother's having or what other kids are having. So we've just said, okay, when we're having soda as a treat, we'll have it once in a while and you can have a diet soda. Yesterday at the restaurant, the waitress is taking our orders and my son says, I'll have a diet soda, or I'll have a decaf coffee, things that don't have sugar and are very easy to manage and he loves. And they're just like, they look at me like very confused. And then I feel like I have to explain, oh, he has diabetes, that's why we're ordering this. We're not crazy parents letting our kids have aspartame and coffee.

Interviewer 1: Really interesting. Thank you. Thank you for that. That really makes me think about how our old notions of diabetes, which were more prevalent then, are still deeply prevalent in the wider community because the education is well, maybe not there, I don't know what to say. I was gonna say it's not there, people just don't have the knowledge.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The parent describes feeling judged by others for dietary choices, perceiving negative attitudes and confusion from others, and feeling compelled to justify decisions. The child feels different

input text:
Interviewer 2: In? I was just wondering on that note, you know, how in an organization that's focused on diabetes, how prevalent conversation is amongst your peers and colleagues. Because I know one of the things we're really looking at doing is bringing experience of interactions and conversations and this experience of stigma into how the entire system responds.

PAD-Parent of T1 Child : I talk about it often because my direct coworker is also a mom of a type one. So I don't know if my experience is the same as all. But I'm often amazed at how accepting my coworkers who do not live with diabetes are. They're like, take all the time you need, don't worry how long it takes. Or when I've had a bad night and we're just chit-chatting before a meeting, they can feel empathy and willingness to learn about my night and why did that happen? It's always there, right? There are a lot of people working at Diabetes Canada who do not live with diabetes.

I'm always astounded at how you have no idea how this impacts people yet here you are trying to make a difference. And yeah, I think it is hard to change the conversation. We work on changing the term diabetic and all the stigma associated with that word. So I myself have changed my language. Some coworkers are also changing their language, but it's hard. But I think it's an important piece of changing the conversation because it's a loaded word. When you say, my son is a person living with diabetes, empathy naturally comes. Whereas you say, my son's a diabetic, and it's like, oh, is your son 180 pounds? And like, is he eating hamburgers and drinking pop every day? It's just totally different when we use different language.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The speaker discusses negative stereotypes linked to the word "diabetic," such as assumptions about weight and diet, reflecting perceived and internalized stigma. The mention of weight

input text:
Interviewer 1: No, I'm glad I asked the question because now I'll bring it back to change the conversation. How do we change the conversation with folks and is it really only about the money, but are there other things that we can do to change the conversation locally, regionally, provincially, nationally? Like what does that look like from where you sit?
PAD-Parent of T1 Child: I think it's a grassroots effort. It can't come from the top. Some of it will help trickle down, but healthcare providers need to be trained in the language and how patients and their caregivers are truly affected by a diagnosis of type one or type two. And addressing and validating how they're feeling based on everything society has told them about diabetes is key. Just talking to them about how it's false can make a difference. Having a healthcare provider, someone you admire and assume knows everything, tell you that no, you didn't get type one because your parents let you have soda at every birthday party or type two because you didn't exercise five times a week is crucial. I don't know how else to do it other than at the grassroots level.

input text:
[Interviewer 1]:: Great. Okay. Sorry for all the stuff upfront, but I want to learn how to do this and so we're practicing. We wanna learn about your experience of stigma and diabetes, and I wonder whether you've had time to think about it, whether you need me to prompt you anymore or whether you want to just tell us some stories of your experience.
[PWT1D]: Yeah, well I've thought of it a little bit and a couple key moments came up. But of course I might be short with what I say, so feel free to have follow-ups and continue to push. So there's been times, and this is probably the most frequent one, when people say you became a type one because you ate too much sugar as a kid, or because you had too much sugar. And so it's a fairly common refrain. I think it's just largely because they don't understand the mechanisms of type one. We don't fully understand it either, but they don't understand it. They just assume that anyone who gets diabetes is because they had way too much sugar and that's what happens. So there's just a lack of education, at least amongst a lot of the people that have said that to me over the years.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The speaker describes being told by others that they developed type 1 diabetes due to eating too much sugar, reflecting direct negative stereotypes (experienced stigma) and awareness of these misconceptions in society

input text:
[Interviewer 1]:: So, I was a diabetes researcher for a hundred years, it feels like, and I know about both type one and type two. And it actually surprises me that people with type one have the experience of being stigmatized for eating too much sugar. I don't because I know too much about the mechanisms. And so she goes, so you have diabetes. And at that moment, I think two things. I felt like I'd failed and I felt like I wasn't understood.
And then yesterday, I was at a ceremony for the Diabetes Hope Foundation, which is a small family foundation that gives scholarships to kids with type one diabetes. It's a very big thing, but it's exactly what you said - I yearn for ways that people could get reeducated about diabetes, right? And I don't know what that looks like, but I'm thinking of that lunch with my friend Peter. He's a good egg. Like, he wasn't coming, but people are good eggs who also say racist things and like, people. So it's around how we could change the basic understanding about that. The trick with diabetes is, um, I was doing a radio interview when I was president of Diabetes Canada. And a number of the call people were doing it about stigma and a number of the call people were just like, thank you for having this radio show. Like they really don't understand. But then the radio host said, but it does at some point often still tie to weight management and exercise in what you eat, right? Like he goes, I don't wanna be politically incorrect.

And so I think that's the challenge here is because like when I lose weight, my numbers get better, right? And so there is a correlation, whereas for example, in the cancer example I gave you, people with small cell carcinoma often literally have never smoked. Like, so in this case there is a correlation, but how do we reeducate about diabetes? And at a basic level, like, you know, it means things are going wrong with your pancreas, right? Like almost a little bit not more scientific, but understanding what's happening.

But cuz when I find, when I explain that way, people go, oh, like it's just not somehow you're fat or you don't exercise and therefore your blood sugar. Like they actually understand, oh, something's going wrong with your pancreas and in some cases the pancreas stops giving. So that's kind of type one. And in other cases it's just not doing it very well. Oh, I've never heard it described that way. That's interesting. So you just have an organ that's not functioning. Exactly. Well that's not totally your fault.

I'm like, no, exactly. Like, so I think at some level, but how do we also not in a sense detach from reality? Like, so when that interviewer said it is tied, I go, of course it's tied like even in my own diabetes, if I can eat well exercise more, manage my weight more, um, my diabetes gets better. Then the question though is so why don't you just do all that all the time? Right? And that's where, um, I say to folks, behavior change is hard.

Okay, so in my perfect world, if I could be a skinny size 10 all the time, like do I want to, yes. The amount of work and as I get older to do that and what my genes allow and what other people's genes allow, like it is different. But again, ha it's very complicated messaging. And what I would never want is somehow, which does seem to be in the obesity group a little bit like any weight is a healthy weight. That's not what I'm like, you know.

I think even something like that, I don't, it's not the right thing or whatever, but it would just be like, I got a retort, I got something. And then people might be like, oh, I didn't realize. Like I ended up with my friend Peter explaining more stuff, but I'm like, why do I have to be scientist 101 here? Like why do I have to? But the reality is people don't understand it, right?

And um, and then they certainly don't understand, like, they're like, oh, how are your needles? And I'm like, well, I'm not on insulin. They're like, oh, I thought all diabetics were on insulin. And um, what I find fascinating is since I started wearing this thing, I'm even at the iab, the institute advisory board for a C H R institute, [Interviewer 2], uh, the institute, nutrition, metabolism and diabetes, that [Interviewer 1] was the scientific director.

And I'm there with people who are experts in their field. And they're like, oh, you're not on insulin? Like, why aren't you? And I'm like, well, it's because of my type one diabetes. And they're like, oh, okay. So that's a good conversation to have. But the other conversations, like when people are just curious and want to learn more, that's where we need to be having those conversations.

Like, in spaces where people feel safe and comfortable asking questions. Like, at the fashion show last weekend, my husband came to support me and he said, okay, I totally get it now. He goes, I couldn't understand it before, but when your mc was describing why people hide their pumps or they hide their monitors and how's ald? And he goes, I get that, that we just don't know, do we?

I said, no, like people don't know. And so how do we have that mind shift that he had and it wasn't like he was at a fashion show, right? Like he was not at a scientific lecture or something like that. And I do wonder whether the big thing that's coming in diabetes and [Interviewer 1], you would know far more about this than I, but like the heterogeneity that's coming in diabetes, right?

This idea that yes, there's type one and type two and gestational, but really this idea of precision medicine like that people, depending on what where you are and what you have, like why do certain people respond to medic? Like, and then there's non-responders. Is there a genetic element to that? Is there an environmental?

I think the more people understand the complexity of diabetes, which is good timing because I think research is getting into the complexity of diabetes. I actually think it provides a tool potentially, [Interviewer 1], to answer your question of, you know, this isn't as simple as you think.

And it's not like these people did wrong things and now they shoot up with insulin <laugh>. Like, there's a lot more going on here. And um, and so I think the heterogeneity piece, um, I think it's just that the reason one might get diabetes is a lot more complex than you think.

input text:
Evelyn: In what way do you think diabetes may affect employment?

Kenneth : Well, there are some basic ways in which it can be very common. Certain jobs you simply cannot do because of physical limitations due to medication. Especially if you're insulin dependent, taking drugs that can impact your performance is a significant issue. However, the drug itself does not directly affect your performance; rather, it's the effects of the insulin on your body that can cause problems. Specifically, in the brain, it can lead to difficulties with decision-making and concentration. For example, even simple tasks like driving may become challenging due to drowsiness caused by the medication. Employers would need to consider hiring additional staff to support individuals with diabetes, as they may require assistance with certain tasks. Nevertheless, if you're proactive about managing your condition and assessing potential risks early on, it's possible to navigate employment successfully. In office settings, I've found that being aware of one's limitations is crucial. For instance, I wouldn't be the best person to serve as a first aid representative because my judgment might be impaired during an emergency. Similarly, operating equipment can also pose challenges.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text suggests that people with diabetes may be less capable at work, need extra support, or pose risks in certain roles. It reflects perceived and anticipated stigma

input text:
Evelyn: I know you mentioned that maybe people would hide their diabetes at work.  Is that something you have experience with?

Evelyn: Tell me about that.

Started school, had the big carrier on my bike so that I could carry papers and all that sort of stuff.  And then in about the third week of July, I got sent to the hospital.  I didn't come back for like almost two months.  And lots of people close to you, like friends and relatives, sisters and brothers and stuff.  They think, well, he can handle it. And then when I got eventually released, and it was really released, I had to go with my parents to the junior high school.  Stand up in front of the principal.  Answer lots and lots of questions and sign lots and lots of papers.  All about things that I will do and things that I won't do.  And I started getting pretty repentant about it.  I thought everybody was picking on me.  I used to have to take my school lunch into the principal's office at one o'clock so that he could check to see that I ate everything I was supposed to.  And that was, it just bugged me.  Why did I have to do that?  They kicked me off the junior rugby team.  A lot of my friends said they were going to quit too, but they never did because the school was pretty good with rugby.  You know, that was not fair. Kenneth : I think. For me. There was the changeover from always feeling that you were like fundraisers or something like that, trying to do something good for a cause. And in many cases can make a mark in your life that says, well, I know this guy, he's a diabetic. I can phone him. I'm having problems with something to do with managing my diabetes. I'll see how he does. And when you start doing something like that, it means you're not on the low end of the stick anymore. Complaining all the time because diabetes can complain. They're probably some of the best complainers in the world. But having these conditions where you have to eat properly and stay away from a lot of types of entertainment. Food and alcohol and that kind of thing that young people are definitely attracted to. But you put yourself at a level that says, I know that if I was to participate in some of these things, my diabetes would be very poor and you probably already experienced some instances where it has been quite poor. And you're very noticeable to everyone that knows you. So you kind of just get your own courage to be yourself, but not be yourself in a bad way. And I'm not saying all diabetics are bad. I'm just saying try to do the right thing. And then over the years as technology got more and more into diabetes, the things that you could do were actually really incredible. That sometimes you didn't even know about it until people are doing it. Like for instance insulin pumps, a good example. In the group that I was with, basically it was brought up because somebody's mother was a diabetic. Apparently, she got an insulin pump. But then when you try to find out if you could get one as a kid, it was no, no, they're not for kids yet. But it changed, it totally changed and became more acceptable. input text:
So have you ever had negative reactions to your dealing or managing diabetes, or any other aspect of it? I feel like I can come up with two, and the first one. I don't know if I would say it was negative. It was negative from my point of view. So we have five-year driver's licenses in Alberta. And I'm going to say, I was diagnosed three years into my five-year driver's license. So I was not aware that as a diabetic, I have to have a driver's medical in order to get a driver's license, so I continued to drive around until my driver's license expired, and then I went to the driver's license office or to the registry, which was like, "Hi, I need to get...like I need to renew my license." So question one. Do you have any heart problems? Like no question. Two. Do you have diabetes? And I was like, yes, and they went well, we can't give you a new license right now, and I was like what. And because that was not something that I was aware I needed to do as a person with diabetes. Then that became an issue, because I was also moving towns that summer, and so I went and got my driver's medical on the driver's medical. One of the questions was, "When was your last low?" I was like well yesterday. And then they denied my license because the question was supposed to be, "When was your last low that required support, or whatever the one is like." When did you need external assistance? And so then I had to call the registry people up and fight them, being like, "No, this is how life with diabetes works." So this was the negative side of it. That I was like, well, I've been driving for two years; I've had no issues as someone with diabetes. Lows are a frequent occurrence, like, I'm going to say, I get one probably every day or every other day. Like, this is just how it works when you're trying to keep your blood sugar within range and trying to get your A1C down; you end up being low. And so that was really crappy. And I basically said to them, like, "You need to change your question so that it says requiring assistance or whatever it is that you're concerned about." And I don't know if it was just a case of driver's medicals not being very common where I lived, and they misread the question. Or if that's actually what the question was; I feel like the last two that I've done, that question has been "When is your last low? That's needed assistance or whatever they phrase it." So whether I was instrumental in changing that or whether they missed reading the question, I don't know. So I would say, that's probably my first like super negative experience.

And then the only other one I can think of was when I went to the dentist last year. When is your last low? That's needed assistance or whatever they phrase it. As so whether I was instrumental in changing that or whether they missed reading the question, I don't know. So I would say, that's probably my first like super negative experience. So one of the questions is, "Do you have diabetes?" So I'm like, well, yeah, do you take medication? Yes, I take it. But it was a new dental hygienist, and she asked me what my blood sugar was. So I checked, and I told her it was 10, which for me, 10.9 after I've eaten is like a fairly okay number. I didn't see anything wrong with it. But she was very much like, "Oh, that's high," and almost didn't want to clean my teeth because my blood sugar was higher than she thought it should have been. And I felt...I did not like that, and I felt judged, and I felt like whether she didn't understand. And I had said, like, "That's fine; that's a normal blood sugar for somebody with Type one." And she came back with while it's my license on the line or something like that. And then didn't want to do...I don't know. There was something she was supposed to do. I don't remember what it was, and she was like, "Well, we're not doing that today." And I would...I feel like that is the most negative, like the first one was really crappy. But I get that's the form, and it's a government-regulated whatever, and sometimes dealings with the government or questionable. Anyway, this one felt like it was very personal, and that it was like your blood sugar is too high, and I was like, "What do you even understand how this works?" Like, if I were 18, yeah, okay, I'd hear where you were coming from. But like, you can see the rest of the data if you want; like, it's not that high, so I don't know where that came from with her. And hopefully, I don't have her again. Yeah.

And then it was one of those things that I retrospectively was like...I don't know if people are actually allowed to ask you what your blood sugar is for things like that. And I don't remember if I googled it or not. But that's something that I definitely now going to the dentist, I'm going to have in the back of my mind about. Well, do I have to tell you? And maybe next time I'm going to be more argumentative with you. And probably, if it happens again, I will look at switching dentists or whatever...or at least making a comment to them.

input text:
Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her):  thank you for describing that. So how satisfied would you say that you are with your quality of life right now in relation to diabetes.
KB :  I think my quality of life is good because I work hard at it, and I make it good. So I am really on top of my sugars, like I'm constantly checking them. And one of the reasons why I moved to the T. Slim was that it has the control IQ integrated into it so that I can help keep my sugars under control. My last A1C was 6.9 or 7, and the one before that was 7.1. But it kind of fluctuates in that range. And I would say, for probably over 18 years now since I've been on a pump, my A1C hasn't been above 7.5. I'm really diligent about taking care of myself and making sure my sugar is okay and preventative.

When I was pregnant, my A1C was actually 5.8 because I was very careful. So I developed something called obsessive-compulsive disorder, because there's a lot of talk about the negatives of type one diabetes carrying babies and stuff. So anyway, all that to say is that my quality of life is really good because I am really diligent in taking care of myself.

input text: Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her): So. I'd like to understand how people perceive type one diabetes. input text:
Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her):  What were your own misconceptions before you were diagnosed at age 21.
KB :  And so I have a really good friend that has type one diabetes and was diagnosed when we were kids. And so I already knew lots about type one diabetes. So I don't know that I had any misconceptions about it other than I didn't realize how mentally and emotionally it like that toll that plays with it. II had never thought about that. So I think that was maybe my misconception. But yeah, and how do you think that the media portrays diabetes and people living with diabetes? Not great. I don't know if you've seen the movie Stealing Magnolia or not, but that's sort of the one that comes to mind mostly when I think about the media and how they portrayed it, and how scary and awful that was. And so I had seen that movie when I was a teenager. And so that was also part of the reason why I hyperfixated on my blood sugar levels when I was having a family because of that movie in particular, like you see it, you hear it all the time. But like that movie really played a big part of that. So I think the media could do a lot better job of normalizing it. I mean, I have an American Girl doll, you know. I'm in my forties, but I still bought myself that American Girl Doll because when I was little there was nothing that included diabetes in the toys or dolls, or anything like that, you know, and from Diabetes Express they had a Teddy bear that they used to give to kids to practice their injections on. But I was like, Okay, nobody gave me a Teddy bear when I got diagnosed with type one, you know, and I was like, I'm going to buy myself that bear. So I have the bear and a happy American Girl doll, and I have an 8-year-old daughter. So she plays with them like the American Girl. I'm not sure if you're familiar with it or not, but it has a little insulin pump, and it has a CGM, and stuff so it just normalizes it a little bit more. And I think that is important and should be more in the media so that people aren't thinking it's like, you know, there are a lot of us with type one diabetes. So we're all pretty much doing okay, you know, like it's not the death sentence, you know, like it's not like the doom and gloom of what they portray it to be in the media.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The speaker discusses negative and frightening portrayals of diabetes in the media, which contribute to public misconceptions and fear. They also mention the lack of normalization in toys and media,

input text: Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her):  And do you normally test your glucose levels and administer insulin with your pump in public? Because I'm much more comfortable now that I'm on the pump when I was first diagnosed, and having to do injections, I would if I was in a restaurant, for example, like I would have gone to the bathroom to give myself my correction. One time in a public bathroom, a lady started screaming and yelling at me about doing drugs in front of her kids. And I was like, "I just started crying." My friend, who's also type one, came out of the stall and tore a strip off of her, but I was only diagnosed at that point for six months. So I was just like, this is all so new to me. And then to have that happen, I was like, "Oh, I'm so embarrassed." After that, I didn't even do it in the bathroom counter; I would go into the stall and shut the door because I was like, "I don't want anyone to see that." Then, after I got my pump, a lady asked me why I carry my garage door opener around with me. I said, "Pardon," and she pointed to my palm because of the Medtronic's blue arrows. She thought it was a garage door opener, but I just really love my car. And then I didn't have the energy or time to educate her about that, you know? But now, honestly, I'm in my 40s, and I don't care what other people think of me now. I wish I had that confidence when I was younger, which I have now, and I just think dealing with it for over 20 years has given me the confidence to do what's necessary. So if they don't like looking at my meter, turn around; don't look. I don't care. I don't test my sugar levels very much anymore because I'm on the Dexcoms. And you know, I've been on the Dexcom since it was Dexcom 4, and I was on the Freestyle Libre before that, and I was on the Medtronic. But anyway, yes, so I was on the CGM for quite a long time, but with the Guardian, I always still had to test my sugar because it was never accurate. I didn't find the Libra very accurate for me either. I understand that the 2 is much more accurate, and you're not having to scan, and it goes back further and all that. But now, like, I don't have to be like, "Oh, with my phone or pricking my finger." If I had to, I still have a test kit in my desk drawer at work, like apple juice, glucose tablets, and I have a whole little stash there. I have it in my car, one at home, one at the cottage, like I have stuff everywhere, just in case. So if I'm if I have to do it, I'm gonna do it, and I don't care what other people think now.
label: Yes stigma

input text:
Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her):  This workplace is very supportive, not so much in previous ones. But okay, well, we will move on to the workplace later on. And I'd like to hear those stories. So how would you say that type of diabetes has impacted your relationships with friends, family partners?I don't think it's had any effect on my relationships.

KB:  My relationship with my family or friends was definitely impacted by my spouse. We are now divorced. One of the things he said at the end when we were getting a divorce was because my health issues were too much for him to handle. I got diagnosed with cancer, and I feel like it wasn't fair of him to say that because he never had to intervene not once. When I was first diagnosed, there were some troubling times of crazy highs, crazy lows, and just trying to manage that. I lost consciousness a couple of times, and there were a lot of times where my parents had to be involved in managing my diabetes.

But none of that has to happen anymore because my diabetes is well managed now. So anyway. But how do you deal with diabetes in new relationships, whether it's a new friend or potential new partner? I tell people right away. If we're going for a meal, like on a date, then I'll just find it comes up organically. They'll be like, "Oh, it's not a big deal," and they'll ask me to explain how I manage my diabetes.

Most of the time, it's been that way, and we just have a conversation about it. Same with my friends if we're going out. I would tell them right away so that everybody's aware. It's kind of like my own safety plan, too. If I'm running or doing something physical, I'll let them know what to do in case of an emergency.

I also have a tracking app called Life 360, which allows my family and friends to see where I am at all times. So if I'm out for a run, they can pop in and see where I am. If I'm running late or need help, they'll know exactly where I am. So anyway.

KB:  Have you ever felt excluded from any social activities because of your health issues?

input text:
Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her):  excellent. And you told me that you had a previous job where the situation seemed to be different. Can you tell me about that?
KB : He used to work for the bank, and my boss was not at all supportive. I had a low sugar one time we were in a meeting, and he's like, "Can you do that after this is over? It's really disruptive." And I was like, "Well, I could die if that would make you feel better." Little bit stark about it. But I was just like, "I have to treat my low sugar." I can't just sit here and let it go down. So I had a low sugar one time we were in a meeting, and he's like, "Can you do that after this is over? It's really disruptive." And I was like, "Well, I could die if that would make you feel better." It'll just keep going down if I don't treat it right. So yeah. And he was just being unreasonable. We had a no food policy at our desks, including coffee. You could only have water at your desk, and I was like, "I need to have glucose tablets. They're not food, but they're like emergency medication." I need to have them with me. And he's like, "You need to provide me with a doctor's note stating that you need that as an accommodation." It was very okay, no problem. Here's the thing: we weren't allowed to have personal cell phones at work. And I had just connected my phone to my personal phone, so I needed to have it with me in order to receive calls and messages. So I got a note from my doctor to say that it was used as a medical device, and that I needed to carry my personal phone with me. He's like, "I want it silenced. I don't want to hear it." If I have a low sugar, the alarm will go off, and it's an audible loud alarm to make sure that I'm safe. So if it went off in a meeting, he'd be like, "You know, roll his eyes and get frustrated." And I was like, "I'm really sorry, but it doesn't bother me." But like it, probably I worked for him for a year, and I would say I probably only had maybe 5 low sugars in that whole year while I was at work. It wasn't like it was happening all the time, but every time it happened he made a big deal about it, and then he actually sent me to HR to have a meeting with them about my health and wellness, to make sure that I was properly taking care of myself. Because if I was managing my diabetes correctly, I wouldn't be having any low sugars. So how does that make you feel? I quit in that meeting. I was like, "I'm done." And I actually filed a human rights tribunal complaint with an employment lawyer, because I said, "I quit because I felt like I had no other choice. They made me feel like if I didn't quit, they were gonna fire me over it." So I quit, I said, because it was nerve-wracking to go into a meeting and not know what would happen if I have a low sugar. He's gonna freak out and then fire me over it, you know? So yeah. So that company got fined. And I think people need to speak up more when those kind of things happen. People often would just walk away and say, "Oh, I don't wanna deal with it." But then I thought like, what if somebody else happened in a different organization? Like thousands and thousands of employees? Right? And he was not just any boss; he was a senior executive vice president who oversaw hundreds of people. So I said, "I'm probably not the only diabetic he deals with on a daily basis."
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The speaker describes direct discrimination (Experienced) from a boss who was unsupportive and punitive about diabetes management. They anticipated further stigma and job loss

input text: So how did that make you feel when you got that attitude from him? I just felt like I was being blamed for not taking care of myself. It was like my fault that it was having a low sugar, but sometimes they just happen. If I have a headache, then my sugars go wonky. If I'm tired because I didn't sleep right the night before, my sugars will get wonky. If we have a deadline coming up and I'm stressed, my sugars can drop too. But with the dexcom now, it's even more progress because it can catch when it starts to drop in rate, and the control helps with that. So I definitely have fewer low sugars than I would have or trending down. However, I always had my alarm set so if it dropped below 4.5, it would alert me, and I didn't want to have a low sugar. Even though it wasn't necessarily having a low sugar where someone would have to intervene, that was still taking care of myself. It felt like I was being targeted or punished for putting my health first, which doesn't feel nice. So it was very stressful. And it was like a huge weight being lifted off my shoulders when I quit because I didn't have to deal with that anymore.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The speaker describes feeling blamed and targeted for diabetes management, indicating experienced stigma. They perceive others’ negative attitudes and internalize blame, feeling punished for prioritizing their health

input text:
Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her):  I want to talk about how you've been treated in the healthcare system, considering the time we have available. We might run over by five minutes. Do you have an extra five minutes? That's great. Thank you. So first of all, can you explain what types of healthcare providers you deal with, either for diving specifically or any other ongoing issues?

KB:  I see a lot of doctors. Okay? For my diabetes, I see my endocrinologist twice a year and the Diabetes Education Center. I also see a nurse and a dietician once a year. Additionally, I have a family doctor who doesn't directly manage my diabetes but sends me for additional blood work to ensure my cholesterol levels are okay.

My iron levels are fine, and he's just checking on that. So every three months, I visit one of those doctors for blood work, including looking at my A1C. I had thyroid cancer, so I see the endocrinologist for that as well. And when I was pregnant with twins, I lost my son at birth, but my daughter was okay. They did a lot of testing because my spouse blamed my diabetes for the loss of our son. The doctor said it wasn't related to my diabetes.

I also see a psychiatrist and a social worker therapist due to post-traumatic stress disorder, postpartum obsessive-compulsive disorder, and postpartum depression. Two years ago, I was diagnosed with endometrial carcinoma, which is cancer of the endometrial lining. I had a hysterectomy, where they removed my Fallopian tubes, ovaries, cervix, uterus, and other parts.

I see an oncology gynecologist for that as well and go to the women's health clinic because I'm at high risk for breast cancer due to having thyroid cancer and endometrial cancer. They do regular screening with an MRI mammogram, CT scan, and ultrasound.

How has your experience been with diabetes in the healthcare system? You think? Being in the hospital many times, I can say that the nurses often don't understand type one diabetes. They just assume you need to eat bland food and limit carbs, which is awful. So I try not to do that. My gynecological oncologist was great when I had my surgery.

She consulted with my endocrinologist, and they arranged for another endocrinologist on site. They left my pump on and let me monitor my blood sugar levels using my own tester. Those experiences have been good other than the food and things. And sometimes, yeah, I would say that the doctors have been great, but the nursing not so much.

If I've ever had to go to the ER for something like a car accident or whatever, they often don't know what type one diabetes is. They're like, "Oh, okay, well, what's this?" Can we take that off? No, please don't take my Cgm. Like my knees are injured, not my arm.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text describes direct experiences of healthcare staff lacking understanding of type 1 diabetes, making incorrect assumptions about diet, and questioning diabetes devices, which reflects both experienced stigma (mismanagement,

input text:
Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her):  So can you think about a time when you or somebody in your life changed their minds about a diabetes stereotype? Do you have any example that comes to mind. My brother was like, "Can you think about a time when you or somebody in your life changed their minds about a diabetes stereotype?" Do you have any example that comes to mind.

KB:  Yeah. So when I was first diagnosed, I said to you like, I had to stop playing hockey. I was in the United States for school. I had to come home to Canada because my healthcare insurance didn't cover all that was going to be required for my type one diagnosis, and they wanted me to stay in the hospital for two weeks. They actually flew me home so I could go right to the hospital here. Anyway, my brother was like, "Oh, you can't play hockey again. You can't do this. You can't do that." He went into big brother mode of being super protective and said, "You're a fragile little flower; you can't do anything because you're type one diabetic." And oh, you can't eat that, you can't... He was very protective.

I was very sick when I got diagnosed. I lost a lot of weight very quickly. I had bronchitis, and like I had cold and sinus issues. In fact, I was very sick. But then they figured out that's what triggered the autoimmune reaction with that. And so I got diagnosed. I was really sick at the beginning of September, and I got diagnosed in November, and I had lost over 30 pounds in that time. I was already super fit because I was playing hockey, but I was fainting, and things, and I had trouble waking up. I was really drowsy all the time; my sugar was 54 when I got diagnosed in the hospital. The doctor said, "I don't know how you're still breathing." But then they put me on insulin right away.

So you know, then they were calling my parents up in Canada to tell them everything. And then, like my whole family's in the room listening to this. So my brother was very concerned; he thought I was going to die. It was for him to see me taking care of myself, eating healthy, gaining weight back, getting color coming back in sleeping okay, feeling okay... And then I said, "Let's go for a skate." You know, hockey's life. So we went out skating, and he kept checking my sugar every 10 minutes. He was like, "Okay, you're doing okay." And now I still play hockey, and I coach hockey.

For him to see me taking care of myself, eating healthy, gaining weight back... That was a big change for him. He's probably my biggest supporter. Not just that; he's also my running coach. So when I signed up to run my first marathon, he said, "You're doing the half-marathon? No, no, you're doing the full marathon." Let's go bigger! You can do anything now. He actually bought me a shirt that says, "I can do anything but make insulin." That was his way of saying, "Don't let diabetes stop you; your diabetes isn't going to stop you from doing anything."
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The text describes the brother’s initial belief that the speaker was "fragile" and unable to play hockey or do normal activities due to type 1 diabetes,

input text:
Sylvie Croteau - dQ&A (She/Her):  So you're talking about people's misconceptions today. What were your own misconceptions before you were diagnosed.
RB :  Honestly, I didn't really have that many misconceptions. I would say that I didn't understand the amount of effort that would go into it. But it's just because something that I'd never thought about. I did have a second cousin with type one diabetes since childhood, and I never gave any kind of thought to the amount of effort he has to put in to control his condition. You know, people like me hear others talking about how they're not doing this or that, and I think many people don't understand the mental toll it takes. They just do not understand it unless they're going through it themselves. It's a constant pressure from yourself and sometimes from your healthcare provider, but not always. Sometimes it feels like society as a whole is putting pressure on you to be perfect. Because if something goes wrong, people are gonna say that you didn't take care of yourself. I know this because I've heard people say that before. For example, I have severe diabetic retinopathy and painful diabetic neuropathy, both of which developed within the first three months of my diagnosis. These complications aren't just physical; they're also a result of the mental toll that's been taken on me. It's mentally exhausting sometimes to live with this condition and try to stay healthy.
label: Yes stigma
reasoning: The speaker describes societal and self-imposed pressure to be "perfect," fear of being blamed for complications, and awareness of negative judgments from others. This reflects

input text:

RB :  So it wrecked it.  I probably only actually worked for six to nine months after I got it. And then I basically had to stop because I have three or four naps every afternoon. It became possible to work, but it's frustrating. Because it cost me a ton of money. You know, I basically had to stop working just as I was hitting my stride at the age of 30, and people definitely discriminate and voice their opinions about how you can afford things like that. If I didn't work, I would lose everything. They don't understand insurance or financial planning, which is what I did before I got sick. And I'd managed to work my way up relatively quickly. So I loved working. And so that was a big thing to get over. Therapists and things didn't help because when you're 30 years old and can't work, all they care about is getting you back to work, which is abnormal for someone that age. That did more harm than good, I would say at that point. And so it took a while to just accept that working isn't going to be one of those things. And there are people who say, "Oh, that sucks, you've missed out on a lot." But then there are people who say, "I wish I could just stay home and do all the fun stuff." So it's not like being healthy means you get to stay home and do nothing.
